title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript provides a theoretical study of the so-called “label trick,” a technique that allows simultaneous propagation of labels and node features in state-of-the-art GNNs. The authors propose a simplified, self-excluded version of the label trick via an extreme splitting scheme, then demonstrate that random label splits can be interpreted as a form of regularization on the self-excluded label weights. Beyond theory, they outline two broader applications: (a) equipping graph methods with trainable label-weight parameters and (b) eliminating randomness by embedding self-excluded label propagation directly into linear propagation GNN layers.

Detailed Comments  
1.  Since prior literature already shows that the label trick can boost GNN performance, I appreciate that the paper does not simply rehash those experiments but instead explores new application scenarios in Section 4. Unfortunately, the empirical evidence in Sections 5.1–5.4 (see Tables 1–4) suggests minimal or no clear advantage over baseline models. In most cases, the gains are within the reported standard deviations across ten independent runs, making it hard to conclude that these new uses of the label trick meaningfully improve results.  

2.  I’d like to see a deeper investigation into the role the label trick plays during training. The authors note—quite correctly—that many top entries on major benchmarks (including the top 3 in the KDDCUP 2021 MAG240M-LSC challenge) all employ the label trick. However, this observation alone does not establish that the trick directly yields higher accuracy. To substantiate such a claim, an ablation comparing leading models with and without the label trick is needed. Alternatively, are there other benefits (e.g., faster convergence, greater training stability) that have driven its widespread adoption?

Overall Assessment  
The paper offers a solid formalization and theoretical insight into the label trick, which is its main strength. However, the proposed novel applications are empirically underwhelming, and the experimental evaluation of the label trick’s impact remains incomplete.

Rating  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.
reviews.main_review: The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
The manuscript delivers a comprehensive theoretical underpinning for the recently proposed “label trick,” whereby a random subset of training node labels is used as additional input features. Beginning with the simplest version of this trick, the authors systematically derive its general form as a regularizer. Building on that analysis, they suggest new applications of the label trick in a variety of broader settings.

Detailed Review:  
This contribution is noteworthy for its solid, rigorous formulation and in-depth theoretical justification. The authors present a clear derivation of the label trick as a regularization technique, covering both regression and classification scenarios. Their exposition flows naturally, leading to several thought-provoking observations and conclusions. Motivated by the theoretical results, the paper then proposes novel use cases for the label trick, and empirical evidence indicates these extensions are both interesting and promising. Overall, the analysis is thorough, and the proposed broader applications are compelling. My recommendation is to accept, though I acknowledge that my limited familiarity with some aspects of this topic may affect the depth of my critique.

Summary of Evaluation:  
A rigorous and stimulating theoretical study that introduces well-motivated applications of the label trick, supported by encouraging experimental results.

Rating:  
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This manuscript investigates how training labels can be incorporated into graph neural networks (and, conversely, how node attributes can be used within collective classification schemes like label propagation) to improve node-level predictions. The core idea is the stochastic “label trick,” where true labels are appended to node features and then randomly masked during training to prevent the model from simply learning an identity mapping. Under the simplifying assumption that nodes have no features, the authors demonstrate that this procedure is equivalent to optimizing a sum of a data-fitting term and a regularizer. They then leverage this insight to propose several practical algorithms.

Main Review:

Strengths (+):  
+ The topic—bridging classical label propagation and modern message-passing GNNs—is both timely and of clear interest to the community.  
+ The paper is well motivated: it identifies the identity-mapping pathology of the label trick and proposes a straightforward remedy by zeroing out diagonal entries in the propagation matrix, which aligns well with intuition and is nicely captured in Equation (10).  
+ The work offers a more principled treatment of the label trick, which until now has largely been applied as an ad hoc heuristic. A deeper theoretical understanding of this mechanism is certainly valuable.

Weaknesses (–):  
– The authors should acknowledge and discuss the prior work in https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf, which I believe first introduced this form of the label trick.  
– It is not clear why Equation (3) is formulated as post-multiplying the label matrix Yₜᵣ by a trainable weight W. What intuition justifies expressing the column space of Yₜᵣ in this way? A similar question applies to the learnable smoothing step in the Correct & Smooth framework.  
– Since the paper has a theoretical bent, it would be helpful to compare against other analyses of label versus feature propagation—even when not used jointly—such as Jia & Benson (2021, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).  
– Several equations are difficult to parse. In particular, the notation for E_{splits} is vague. I would appreciate clarification of Equation (5): how exactly does one move from the penultimate expression to the final term? If D_out has size one, shouldn’t the expectation reduce to a uniform average over i ∈ Dₜᵣ, i.e.,  
  (1/n) ∑_{i ∈ Dₜᵣ} ℓ(yᵢ, [P(Yₜᵣ – YᵢW)]ᵢ)?  
  I might be missing something—please elaborate.  
– The manuscript is occasionally hard to follow because of dense notation (excessive subscripts) and the way the “Remarks” are interleaved. A careful rewrite to improve readability would be beneficial.  
– In Table 2, a regression metric such as R² would be more appropriate than the current measure.  
– Section 4.1 lacks sufficient detail. How is that model trained in practice? What is the final learned function—do you simply supervise a truncated Neumann series with a free weight matrix instead of a fixed propagation matrix? Also, please cite existing work on learning or adapting adjacency/propagation matrices.

Questions for the Authors:  
• In the right-hand plot of Figure 1, why is the deterministic version of the label trick not shown as a baseline? Also, which GNN architecture was employed in these experiments? This detail seems missing in Section 5.3.  
• In Remark 4, the authors state that disabling regularization eliminates any chance of overfitting. Could you clarify why that would be the case?  
• The derivation suggests that in the deterministic label-trick scenario one can decompose the objective with an explicit regularizer. Is that formulation actually used to generate the results in Table 3? If not, why was it omitted?

Minor Points (Typographical/Nitpicks):  
– In Remark 2, change “insure” to “ensure.”  
– There is inconsistency in bracket usage between Equations (4) and (5) (parentheses vs. square brackets).

Overall Assessment:  
This paper tackles a significant problem and is clearly motivated, but it requires a number of clarifications, additional comparisons, and notational cleanup before it can meet the bar for publication.

Recommendation:  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.

Main Review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This work examines the so-called “label trick” in graph‐based semi-supervised learning. In this scenario, we have a set of labeled nodes and a set of unlabeled nodes, and the aim is to infer labels for the latter. These nodes form a graph—often weighted—based on feature similarity. A classic approach diffuses known labels through the graph Laplacian, while a more recent strategy constructs a graph neural network (GNN) and learns its parameters to predict node labels. The authors argue that label diffusion relies on labels, and GNNs exploit features, and that the label trick effectively combines both. Concretely, one feeds both node features and known labels into the GNN, but to prevent the network from trivially copying its label inputs, some ground-truth labels are randomly hidden during training. This hiding procedure is the label trick. The paper seeks to demystify this heuristic via analysis of simplified models and illustrates one novel application scenario.

Strengths  
- I appreciate the authors’ effort to step back from empirical wizardry and provide insight into a widely used practical technique through toy models.  
- The manuscript is clearly written and generally easy to follow.

Weaknesses  
- The paper’s findings are not entirely convincing. Its motivations and background are underdeveloped, and the claimed correspondence between the stochastic label trick and a deterministic regularizer lacks direct experimental support.  
- The presentation often lacks rigor (details follow).  
- Some assertions strike me as problematic. For instance, the authors claim graph-Laplacian methods do not leverage features, yet building the graph itself usually depends on feature similarity—albeit unsupervised. Moreover, if features are noisy, the graph edges (also derived from those features) will likewise be noisy, affecting both Laplacian diffusion and GNN performance.  
- Despite its ambition to offer theoretical insight, the paper leaves open key questions: Why does self-excluded prediction underperform compared to the full label trick? And why does the label trick outperform alternatives such as GNNs with Laplacian regularization (e.g., Pfau et al., Cabannes et al.) or MixUp-style regularization (e.g., Lopez-Paz et al.)?

Detailed Comments  

Page 1  
• The exposition would benefit from greater conciseness and precision. For example, in line 4 of the introduction, equating smoothness over the graph with i.i.d. sample assumptions only confuses the reader. Cleanly separating these two points would improve clarity.  
• In the abstract, referring to an “identity mapping from input to output” is unclear. It seems the authors mean the transductive setting where feature X is ignored and labels propagate via a possibly trivial rule (see Eq. (3)). As written, the notion of an identity map from R^d to R^c is ill-posed.

Page 2  
• The graph construction deserves a clearer formal definition. Each node corresponds to a feature vector x ∈ R^d and a one-hot label y ∈ R^c, yielding a design matrix X and label matrix Y. The undirected weighted graph has adjacency matrix S (symmetric by construction).  
• The statement “nodes connected by an edge … are likely to share labels” should specify whether the graph is weighted or unweighted and how edge weights affect this assumption.  
• In Eq. (1), the second matrix F should be F_tr (training predictions) rather than the full F, otherwise a Pythagorean argument implies an unintended penalty driving F on test nodes toward zero. Also, since Y = 0 may carry information (e.g., logits), explaining why pushing F to zero does not bias the solution is important. Finally, note that F ∈ R^{n×c} and that, absent constraints on F, this minimization decouples across each of the c label dimensions.

Page 3  
• The claim “P ≈ P Y” in the second paragraph is incorrect. In fact, since λS is a contraction, one can express P exactly as an infinite series (converging in any matrix norm).  
• The comment “where … P smooths the training labels” should follow the definition P = (1 − λ)(I − λS)^{-1}, because P is not used in the preceding construction of F^(k).

Page 4  
• Remove the stray parenthesis in “model from (…)”.  
• A brief remark on Simplified Graph Convolutions (SGC) would be welcome, along with justification for its inclusion. You could free up space by condensing Sections 3.2 and 3.3.  
• In Eq. (3), it is unclear how P is chosen here—earlier P was introduced as “a propagation matrix,” but no rule for fixing it is provided.

Appendix  
• Several pages display overfull boxes; please inspect the LaTeX log and correct layout issues.

References  
• Convert ArXiv entries to their published conference or journal versions where available.

Overall Assessment  
While it is valuable to explain why practitioners adopt the label trick, this paper does not yet offer a fully satisfactory analysis or a rigorous comparison to other regularization techniques. I believe this line of inquiry is important but that the current manuscript needs more depth and precise argumentation before it is ready for publication.

Rating  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.
reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This manuscript introduces a deterministic variant of the so-called “label trick” for graph neural networks and related label propagation frameworks. By incorporating a self-exclusion operator into the message-passing step, the method aims to eliminate label leakage in a fully deterministic way. The authors further discuss how this approach can be applied to other label propagation scenarios and present experiments comparing (i) classical label propagation with and without the label trick, and (ii) the effect of the regularization term in the proposed deterministic scheme versus its randomized counterpart.

Main Review:

Strengths:  
1. The paper proposes a novel framework for GNNs in which the commonly used stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic mechanism that explicitly prevents self-propagation—an idea I have not seen before.  
2. Establishing an upper bound (Theorem 2) on the cross-entropy loss for the deterministic scheme is a meaningful theoretical contribution.

Weaknesses:  
1. Motivation remains unclear. Aside from sampling considerations, why replace the randomized label trick with a deterministic version? Is there a computational or implementation bottleneck being addressed?  
2. Although the authors state that previous uses of the label trick lack theoretical justification, it is not spelled out what practical benefits (e.g., accuracy gains, faster convergence) the deterministic version delivers.  
3. The experimental comparisons are puzzling. Apart from the plot in Figure 1, there is no direct head-to-head evaluation of the proposed deterministic trick against the state-of-the-art randomized label tricks. Instead, the paper reports on trainable vs. non-trainable propagation and presence vs. absence of any label trick—none of which directly validate the core claim. Why was a direct comparison with SOTA random methods omitted?  
4. Even within the reported experiments, the results for the trainable propagation and label-trick variants show negligible improvements once standard deviations are taken into account. This lack of statistically significant gains undermines the claim that the new approach is advantageous.  
5. Several relevant works on graph-based label propagation and label regularization are missing. In particular:  
   a. Szlam, A. D., Maggioni, M., & Coifman, R. R. (2008). “Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes,” Applied and Computational Harmonic Analysis, 9(55):1711–1739.  
6. The presentation of certain algorithms is not sufficiently self-contained. Readers may struggle to follow some methods, and the reference “C&S” is never properly defined.

Summary of the Review:  
While the paper offers an interesting deterministic twist on a known GNN technique and provides a neat theoretical bound, its core motivation is weakly articulated and the empirical validation is neither comprehensive nor convincing. These shortcomings lead me to recommend against acceptance in its current form.

Rating:  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?

On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.

reviews.text:
Paper Summary:
This manuscript delivers a theoretical treatment of imitation learning (IL) under the assumption of a deterministic expert. It demonstrates that, in this setting, IL can be reformulated as reinforcement learning (RL) with a time‐invariant reward function, and that this stationary reward exactly minimizes the total variation distance between the expert’s and learner’s policies. The authors also relate this reward formulation to f‐divergence objectives frequently employed in adversarial IL. A series of experiments are provided to corroborate the theoretical findings.

Main Review:

Strengths:
1. The paper rigorously establishes that for deterministic experts, IL reduces to RL with a fixed reward signal. This result unifies and theoretically underpins a number of existing IL approaches.
2. It further shows that the stationary reward corresponds to minimizing total variation distance, clarifying the connection to f‐divergence minimization seen in adversarial methods.
3. The proofs are well motivated, clearly structured, and accessible to readers.
4. Empirical studies examine how the amount of expert data influences IL performance, addressing a practically relevant question.

Weaknesses:
1. In implementation, the authors adopt a softened reward of 1 minus the clipped L₂ distance to avoid sparsity. While intuitively reasonable, this choice violates the deterministic‐expert assumption central to the theory. The manuscript should discuss how introducing this soft reward affects the theoretical guarantees.
2. The experimental evaluation is somewhat narrow. For example, the SAC‐based experts appear to underperform relative to past benchmarks, raising the question of whether the same trends would hold with stronger experts (e.g., those from https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Additionally, details of expert training—such as action constraints in SAC versus post‐hoc clipping in a Gaussian policy—can substantially influence IL outcomes. Expanding the experimental suite to address these factors would strengthen the empirical support for the theory.

Questions for the Authors:
1. Could you clarify your motivation for using the 1 – min L₂ reward instead of the reward proposed by Wang et al. (2019)? How do these reward choices compare in practice?
2. Please elaborate on the deterministic expert assumption: How critical is it to your algorithm’s performance? Given only finite data, can one reliably distinguish between a stochastic and a deterministic expert?

Summary of the Review:
This work provides a valuable theoretical contribution by showing that IL with deterministic experts is equivalent to RL with a stationary reward that minimizes total variation distance, thus grounding several existing methods. The connection to f‐divergence objectives is insightful. However, the empirical evaluation is limited in scope, and certain implementation details (soft reward, expert strength, training procedure) require further discussion or experimentation. Overall, despite these issues, the paper offers interesting insights of potential interest to the community.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction
to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. theoretical analysis both
certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance
between the expert and the imitation learner, showing a link to adversarial imitation
learning. Experiments are given to confirm that the reduction works well in
practice for continuous control tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper includes potentially interesting results. Some comments that can improve the paper are given below. 
1) In this paper, average rewards are considered. Is there any reason to consider the average reward MDP instead of discounted MDP, which is more widely used?
2) It is not clear what the norm || ||_{TV} means.
3) It would be better to discuss what kinds of RLs can be used in Proposition 1. It is not clear in the current paper. 
4) It would be better if the definition of intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward is explained in this paper. 
5) It is not clear how the imitation learner can learn the expert policy from the intrinsic reward in section 3. 
It would be better if the process is explained in more details.
6) In the experiment, it is not clear how the comaprative analysis is done. Because the authors did not develop new algorithms, 
it would be better to explain details of the comparative analysis. 
 7) Overall, the organization of the paper can be improved further to more clearly deliver the ideas by adding more detailed backgrounds.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
In sum, the paper seems to contain potentially interesting results. 
The overall organization and presentation can be further improved to improve the readability of the paper.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Manuscript:  
This work demonstrates that, when experts behave deterministically, one can cast imitation learning as a reinforcement learning problem with a fixed reward function. The authors offer theoretical guarantees on recovering the expert’s reward and bound the total variation distance between the expert’s trajectory distribution and that of the learned policy, drawing connections to adversarial imitation approaches. Empirical evaluations on continuous control benchmarks show that this reduction yields strong practical performance.

Detailed Comments:  
1) The paper exclusively focuses on average-reward MDPs. Given the prevalence of discounted-reward formulations in the RL literature, could the authors justify this choice or discuss how their analysis might extend to or differ under a discounted framework?  
2) The notation ‖·‖_{TV} is used to measure divergence between distributions, but it is not explicitly defined. A brief definition or reference would aid clarity.  
3) Proposition 1 hinges on invoking an RL subroutine. It would strengthen the presentation to specify which classes of RL algorithms satisfy the assumptions of this proposition, or at least to outline the required properties.  
4) Terms like “intrinsic reward” and “extrinsic reward” appear throughout Section 3 but are not formally introduced. A concise definition of each would help readers follow the development.  
5) It remains somewhat opaque how the imitation learner actually leverages the intrinsic reward signal to recover the expert’s policy. Expanding on the learning procedure—perhaps with pseudocode or a more detailed walkthrough—would improve comprehension.  
6) In the experimental section, the methodology behind the comparative baselines is not described in depth. Since the core contribution is a reduction rather than a novel algorithm, detailing how baselines were implemented and evaluated is essential for a fair comparison.  
7) Overall, the manuscript could benefit from a more structured organization. Adding a background section that reviews necessary concepts in imitation and adversarial learning, and clearly delineating technical contributions from related work, would make the paper more accessible.

Overall Assessment:  
This paper presents potentially valuable theoretical insights and a practical reduction for imitation learning. However, enhancing the exposition—by defining notation, elaborating on algorithmic details, and clarifying experimental protocols—would substantially improve readability and reproducibility.

Recommendation:  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.
reviews.main_review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

reviews.text:
Rewritten Review

Summary of the Paper:
This manuscript addresses the problem of imitation learning by framing it as a reinforcement learning task with a fixed reward function derived from expert demonstrations. The authors define an intrinsic reward based on expert data and show theoretically that the learned policy achieves high expected per-step intrinsic and extrinsic returns. They also derive high-probability bounds on the divergence between the expert policy and the imitation policy. Empirical evaluations on continuous control benchmarks indicate that their approach performs on par with existing methods, while maintaining algorithmic simplicity.

Detailed Comments:
1. Novelty and Clarity  
   The proposal to cast imitation learning as a standard RL problem with a stationary, dataset-enabled reward is straightforward and well-explained. The paper is clearly written and the theoretical developments are presented in a logically coherent manner.

2. Sparse Reward Concern  
   My primary concern lies with the sparsity of the reward signal. While the intrinsic reward indeed correlates with higher extrinsic performance, sparse feedback often presents severe challenges for any off-the-shelf RL algorithm. In practice, sparse rewards can hinder effective exploration and slow down policy improvement. Could the authors elaborate on how their method overcomes or mitigates these common issues? For instance, are there specific techniques or heuristics they employ to ensure the RL agent makes sufficient progress despite the sparse reward landscape?

3. Computational Efficiency  
   Sparse reward structures typically lead to slower convergence, which raises questions about computational cost. It would strengthen the paper to include runtime benchmarks or convergence-speed comparisons against the baselines. Presenting wall-clock time or number of environment interactions needed to reach comparable performance would help readers assess the practical trade-offs of this approach.

4. Empirical Results  
   The experiments are appropriate and substantiate the theoretical claims. Demonstrating that a simple “reward = 1 if action matches expert, else 0” scheme suffices for existing RL solvers is an appealing contribution. Still, additional details on training stability and sample efficiency would be valuable for practitioners.

Overall Assessment:
This work delivers a rigorous theoretical framework for an easily implementable imitation learning methodology. The idea of using a constant intrinsic reward from expert data is elegant, and the bounds on expected returns provide useful insights. Although the sparse reward raises legitimate concerns about learning speed and sample efficiency, the paper remains a solid and well-articulated contribution with promising implications.

Rating: 6 – marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.

Main Review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors propose an algorithm for imitation learning which rewards the agent for observing state-action pairs that are part of the demonstration-set. The main contribution of the paper is the theoretical analysis which shows that the algorithm, given sufficient expert data, matches the expert’s occupancy distribution and expected reward. The proof comes in three parts: first, the authors relate expert rollouts to the limiting distribution of the expert’s policy. Second, the authors put a probabilistic bound on the expected agreement with expert-data based on the mixing time of the agent. Finally, the authors use this bound on expected agreement to bound the difference in expected extrinsic reward. The authors also provide an empirical evaluation in standard control benchmarks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is generally well written and easy to follow; however, the structure is sometimes unusual. The proofs would be easier to follow if the authors moved Lemmas from Appendix C & D into the main body while moving some of the detailed proofs from the main body into the Appendix.

The derived bound is non-trivial, but it is unclear how significant it is. The main issue lies in the assumption on the amount of data that is required which is linear (with a factor > 1) in the squared size of the state-space (note that the bound remains large even if \delta is set to 1). Such a large amount of expert data is required since rewarding agreement is not a valid strategy when expert data is sparse: imagine a sub-sampled expert trajectory where the agent has the capability to return to previously seen states. In a more typical distribution-matching objective, the agent has to visit all the demonstration states and thus still has to complete the trajectory. In the objective proposed by the authors, the agent can maximize reward by returning to the same demonstration-state repeatedly. Naturally, if demonstrations are available in all states and the agent’s representational capacity is unlimited, then maximizing agreement is trivially optimal. The author’s bound does not require demonstrations to be available in all states and may thus be useful; however, the required amount of expert data is extremely large and no statement can be made if less data is available.

In the related works section, the authors claim that the method is minimizing the GAIL-objective. This is misleading; the GAIL objective is equivalent to minimizing the JS divergence to the expert histogram for all choices of N while the proposed method only minimizes TV distance and thus JS divergence if N is large enough. 

For the empirical evaluation, the authors use an L2-distance as a stand-in for the reward used in the analysis. This is necessary as the reward is undefined in continuous state-action spaces (similarly, it will be too sparse in large discrete state-action spaces), but the implications of that heuristic are not explored in the paper. It would be good if the authors could add motivation and discussion for this form of the reward. The results are very positive, but it is unclear whether they are due to the theoretical properties of the algorithm or due to the particular nature of the mujoco walkers. Since the reward is a part of the observation-space in these domains, using simple heuristics like squared distance can plausibly lead to high performance; however, this would not generalize to other domains. It is furthermore problematic that the hardest benchmark task, humanoid, is missing as the given baseline algorithms have no problem solving it. Finally, the reported numbers for behavioral cloning are significantly worse than what has previously been reported in the literature.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The proposed bound is non-trivial and potentially useful; however, it would benefit from additional context to show whether it is truly significant. The empirical results are interesting, but rely on a heuristic that is not well-motivated and may be domain specific.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

In the related works section, the authors claim that the method is minimizing the GAIL-objective. This is misleading; the GAIL objective is equivalent to minimizing the JS divergence to the expert histogram for all choices of N while the proposed method only minimizes TV distance and thus JS divergence if N is large enough. 

For the empirical evaluation, the authors use an L2-distance as a stand-in for the reward used in the analysis. This is necessary as the reward is undefined in continuous state-action spaces (similarly, it will be too sparse in large discrete state-action spaces), but the implications of that heuristic are not explored in the paper. It would be good if the authors could add motivation and discussion for this form of the reward. The results are very positive, but it is unclear whether they are due to the theoretical properties of the algorithm or due to the particular nature of the mujoco walkers. Since the reward is a part of the observation-space in these domains, using simple heuristics like squared distance can plausibly lead to high performance; however, this would not generalize to other domains. It is furthermore problematic that the hardest benchmark task, humanoid, is missing as the given baseline algorithms have no problem solving it. Finally, the reported numbers for behavioral cloning are significantly worse than what has previously been reported in the literature.

Summary Of The Review:
The proposed bound is non-trivial and potentially useful; however, it would benefit from additional context to show whether it is truly significant. The empirical results are interesting, but rely on a heuristic that is not well-motivated and may be domain specific.
