reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This submission tackles uncertainty calibration in meta-learning for few-shot classification, an area that has received relatively little attention. To address the difficulty of estimating reliable covariance matrices from very few samples, the authors propose to meta-learn these covariances via a set encoder (realized with a Set-Transformer). They also introduce a scaled energy formulation, which parameterizes a logit-normal softmax distribution, aiming to enhance uncertainty calibration in the classification layer.

Strengths  
- The manuscript is written clearly and is easy to follow.  
- Figures and mathematical expressions are well presented and aid understanding.  
- I did not notice any typographical errors.  
- The problem of uncertainty calibration in few-shot meta-learning is both important and underexplored.  
- Experimental results show that the proposed approach improves calibration performance compared to baselines.

Weaknesses  
- My primary reservation concerns the need for an additional meta-learned set encoder to produce task-specific covariance matrices. Prior work has shown that certain meta-learning frameworks inherently offer strong uncertainty quantification without auxiliary components.  
  - For instance, Gaussian process–based meta-learners built on kernel methods [1,2] achieve effective calibration directly.  
- It is unclear whether one could obtain a class-level covariance by aggregating sample-level statistics, thereby eliminating the extra set encoder. For an example of such an aggregation strategy, please see Equations 6–9 in [3], which, although motivated differently, suggest that covariance estimation might be amortized without a dedicated encoder.  
- I encourage the authors to discuss and justify why a separate set encoder is essential, rather than adopting a sample-aggregation approach.

References  
[1] F. Name et al., “Deep Kernel Transfer in Gaussian Processes for Few-Shot Learning,” NeurIPS 2020.  
[2] G. Name et al., “Bayesian Few-Shot Classification with One-vs-Each Pólya-Gamma Augmented Gaussian Processes,” ICLR 2021.  
[3] H. Name et al., “Amortized Bayesian Prototype Meta-Learning: A New Probabilistic Meta-Learning Approach to Few-Shot Image Classification,” AISTATS 2021.

Overall Assessment  
This paper addresses a critical and underexamined challenge in meta-learning and few-shot classification. The quantitative results indicate that the proposed method indeed yields better uncertainty calibration. I recommend that the authors provide a more in-depth justification for the use of an extra meta-learned set encoder.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors proposed methodologies to estimate the covariance matrix of embedded features under the meta-learning setting. The authors first showed that the empirical covariance matrix is inappropriate in this setting and that due to the shift invariant property of softmax function, ProtoNet could produce overly confident predictions. 

The authors instead propose to learn the covariance matrix through a Set Transformer structure, where the covariance matrix can be decomposed into the sum of a diagonal component and a low rank component. The authors used scaled energy and bi-Lipschitz regularization to make the methodology more performant. 

Numerical studies are performed to evaluate the calibration performance as well as the eigenvalue distribution of several methods.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is well written and easy to follow. Some specific comments / suggestions:
1. It would be great if the authors could expand the discussion of bi-Lipschitz regularization in the related work section. Some discussion and literature review of set-input functions, e.g., Set Transformer, Deep Sets will also be helpful.
2. What are the practical ways to ensure that Lambda_c matrix is non-negative, without making too many entries of Lambda_c to be zero?
3. Is there any benchmark on the computational requirement of the proposed methods, compared to competing methods? The covariance inversion and log-determinant will add computational cost to the methods, and it would be good to add some analysis to show the tradeoff between computational cost (i.e., number of iterations) versus performance.
4. In the numerical studies, it seems that the proposal performed comparatively better in the low-way low-shot setting. Is this the correct observation? If so, is there any intuition behind this?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is well-written with clear motivations and easy to follow presentations of the methodology. The proposal is relatively novel, and could be a meaningful contribution to the meta-learning community.

The authors first showed that the empirical covariance matrix is inappropriate in this setting and that due to the shift invariant property of softmax function, ProtoNet could produce overly confident predictions. 

The authors instead propose to learn the covariance matrix through a Set Transformer structure, where the covariance matrix can be decomposed into the sum of a diagonal component and a low rank component. The authors used scaled energy and bi-Lipschitz regularization to make the methodology more performant. 

Numerical studies are performed to evaluate the calibration performance as well as the eigenvalue distribution of several methods.

Main Review:
The paper is well written and easy to follow. Some specific comments / suggestions:
1. It would be great if the authors could expand the discussion of bi-Lipschitz regularization in the related work section. Some discussion and literature review of set-input functions, e.g., Set Transformer, Deep Sets will also be helpful.
2. What are the practical ways to ensure that Lambda_c matrix is non-negative, without making too many entries of Lambda_c to be zero?
3. Is there any benchmark on the computational requirement of the proposed methods, compared to competing methods? The covariance inversion and log-determinant will add computational cost to the methods, and it would be good to add some analysis to show the tradeoff between computational cost (i.e., number of iterations) versus performance.
4. In the numerical studies, it seems that the proposal performed comparatively better in the low-way low-shot setting. Is this the correct observation? If so, is there any intuition behind this?

reviews.text:
Summary of the Manuscript:
This paper addresses the problem of estimating the covariance matrix of feature embeddings in a meta-learning context. The authors first argue that the usual empirical covariance is ill-suited here and that, due to the shift-invariance of the softmax, ProtoNet tends to make overly confident predictions. To remedy this, they introduce a learned covariance parameterization via a Set Transformer, decomposing the covariance into a diagonal part plus a low-rank component. They further incorporate scaled energy and bi-Lipschitz regularization to improve performance. Finally, they conduct numerical experiments assessing calibration quality and eigenvalue spectra across several approaches.

Main Review:
The manuscript is well structured and easy to follow. I offer the following suggestions and questions:

1. Related Work – Bi-Lipschitz and Set Inputs  
   The discussion of bi-Lipschitz regularization could be expanded. In particular, it would be helpful to situate it among other set-input architectures (e.g., Deep Sets, Set Transformer) and discuss relevant literature more thoroughly.

2. Ensuring Nonnegativity of Λ_c  
   The paper should clarify practical mechanisms for enforcing nonnegativity of the Λ_c matrix without driving most entries to zero. What strategies can guarantee a positive-semidefinite structure while retaining sufficient flexibility?

3. Computational Overhead  
   Inverting covariance matrices and computing log-determinants can be computationally intensive. Have the authors benchmarked the runtime or iteration count of their method against competing approaches? Providing some quantitative analysis of the trade-off between computational cost and performance gains would strengthen the evaluation.

4. Performance in Low-Way, Low-Shot Regimes  
   The numerical results suggest that the proposed method yields especially strong improvements in low-way, low-shot settings. Is this indeed the correct interpretation? If so, can the authors offer an intuition for why the method shines in this regime?

Overall Assessment:
The paper is clearly written, with well-motivated ideas and a coherent presentation. The approach appears novel and has the potential to contribute meaningfully to the meta-learning community.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

In this paper, the authors proposed methodologies to estimate the covariance matrix of embedded features under the meta-learning setting. The authors first showed that the empirical covariance matrix is inappropriate in this setting and that due to the shift invariant property of softmax function, ProtoNet could produce overly confident predictions. 

The authors instead propose to learn the covariance matrix through a Set Transformer structure, where the covariance matrix can be decomposed into the sum of a diagonal component and a low rank component. The authors used scaled energy and bi-Lipschitz regularization to make the methodology more performant. 

Numerical studies are performed to evaluate the calibration performance as well as the eigenvalue distribution of several methods.

Main Review:
The paper is well written and easy to follow. Some specific comments / suggestions:
1. It would be great if the authors could expand the discussion of bi-Lipschitz regularization in the related work section. Some discussion and literature review of set-input functions, e.g., Set Transformer, Deep Sets will also be helpful.
2. What are the practical ways to ensure that Lambda_c matrix is non-negative, without making too many entries of Lambda_c to be zero?
3. Is there any benchmark on the computational requirement of the proposed methods, compared to competing methods? The covariance inversion and log-determinant will add computational cost to the methods, and it would be good to add some analysis to show the tradeoff between computational cost (i.e., number of iterations) versus performance.
4. In the numerical studies, it seems that the proposal performed comparatively better in the low-way low-shot setting. Is this the correct observation? If so, is there any intuition behind this?

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is well-written with clear motivations and easy to follow presentations of the methodology. The proposal is relatively novel, and could be a meaningful contribution to the meta-learning community.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This submission focus on improving the performance of uncertainty quantification and OOD detection using bi-Lipschitz regularized neural networks. There are two involved challenges here: (i) we are required to estimate the class-conditional covariance matrix of the latent features. This estimation is challenging under low sample size. (ii) generative classifiers using softmax activation function suffer from shift invariance, and thus may become over-confident for OOD samples.

To resolve these issues, the paper focus on two innovations:

(i) a meta-learning model to estimate the class-conditional covariance matrices,
(ii) a modification of the inference procedure with the energy function to detect OOD samples.

The paper concludes with numerical results showing the performance of the proposed solution approach.
reviews.main_review:
This submission is empirical: the paper is well-motivated, and the paper adapts existing, popular techniques to engineer a solution to the standing problem (using quadratic discriminant analysis, modeling the covariance matrix as a sum of a diagonal matrix and a low-rank psd matrix, etc.).

On the downside, there are several theoretical questions that are left unanswered:
1. Why is the Set Transformer a good choice? On which ground can we justify that the Set Transformer can generate sensible values of Lambda and Phi to construct the covariance matrices?
2. There are a lot of methods from statistics to estimate covariance matrices from low sample size dataset (linear/nonlinear shrinkage, sparse, etc.). Why can't they be used in this application? Is it possible to show that the Set Transformer is better than these traditional approach (for any possible criteria)?
3. In page 8, why should the eigenvalue distribution be diversified? It is well known that the eigenvalue can be sensitive to small perturbations of the off-diagonal entries of the matrix. Thus, what we observed (diversity of the eigenvalues) can be just an artifact of the numerical errors from the Set transformer network. Diverse eigenvalues also imply that the condition number is large, which leads to higher numerical errors to the log-determinant term. Thus, I am not fully convinced that a diverse set of eigenvalues is of any advantage.


Minor:
- What is f(E) and min(E) after equation (10)?
- The abstract and intro focus heavily on bi-Lipschitz regularized NN, however, the exposition in section 3 (except for Section 3.4 which is 10 lines long) relies minimally on this architecture. This emphasis may be misleading in the first read.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
In my opinion, the paper is a good engineering attempt to solve a specific problem. It lacks, unfortunately, concrete scientific contributions.


======== Post-rebuttal ========
The authors have provided further empirical results to justify the use of the ST to estimate the covariance matrix. I have raised my evaluation from 3 to 5.

reviews.text:
Reviewer’s Summary  
This manuscript tackles enhancing uncertainty quantification and out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in bi-Lipschitz regularized neural networks. It confronts two primary difficulties: (i) estimating the class-conditional covariance of latent features with limited samples, and (ii) preventing generative classifiers using softmax from becoming overly confident on shifted (OOD) inputs. To address these, the authors propose (1) a meta-learning approach—based on a Set Transformer—to infer class-specific covariance matrices, and (2) an energy-based adjustment to the inference step for OOD detection. They validate their framework through numerical experiments.

Detailed Comments  
Overall, the paper is a solid empirical contribution: it is well motivated and combines established methods (e.g., quadratic discriminant analysis with covariance modeled as diagonal plus low-rank PSD) into a practical solution. However, several theoretical justifications remain underdeveloped:

1. Rationale for the Set Transformer  
   Why is the Set Transformer particularly suited for predicting the Λ and Φ components of the covariance? The manuscript lacks a principled argument or theoretical guarantee that this architecture yields reliable estimates, rather than any other function approximator.

2. Comparison to Classical Covariance Estimators  
   In statistics, numerous techniques (linear/nonlinear shrinkage, sparse estimation, etc.) exist for reliable covariance estimation under small sample sizes. It would be valuable to know why these classical methods are unsuitable here or how the proposed Set Transformer approach compares, perhaps in terms of estimation error or downstream performance.

3. Interpretation of Eigenvalue Diversity (p. 8)  
   The authors observe that their approach “diversifies” eigenvalues, but it is unclear why this is beneficial. Eigenvalues are notoriously sensitive to perturbations in off-diagonal entries; the observed spread could simply reflect numerical artifacts from the network. Moreover, a wider eigenvalue range implies a larger condition number, which may exacerbate numerical errors in computing log-determinants. I remain unconvinced that greater eigenvalue diversity is advantageous for model performance or stability.

Minor Points  
- After Equation (10), please clarify the definitions of f(E) and min(E).  
- The abstract and introduction emphasize bi-Lipschitz-regularized networks, yet Section 3 barely leverages this architecture except in a brief 10-line subsection (3.4). This framing may mislead readers on the paper’s core technical content.

Overall Assessment  
This work represents a commendable engineering effort to handle specific challenges in uncertainty estimation and OOD detection. Nonetheless, it falls short of delivering significant theoretical insights or demonstrating clear advantages over established statistical methods.

Post-Rebuttal Update  
The authors have provided additional empirical comparisons supporting the choice of the Set Transformer for covariance estimation. In light of this material, I have raised my score from 3 to 5.

Final Rating  
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: ZeroFL: Efficient On-Device Training for  Federated Learning with Local Sparsity

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the problem of adding sparsity to local training in federated learning. Under the approach each node uses only the top-K weights for computations in the forward and backward pass of training (and only top-K activations for computations in the backward pass). The authors empirically show that adding sparsity in this way, while speeding up local training, significantly degrades the accuracy when directly used in federated learning. To mitigate this issue the authors propose to combine sparse training with sparse aggregation since their empirical study suggests that the accuracy drop may be due to dilution of weights that are active in only a few nodes. Experiments on CIFAR10 with non-IID splits using ResNet-18 as the model show some gain in accuracy along with a drop in communication cost when using the proposed schemes.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

1. The authors introduce a new problem of adding sparse local training to federated learning. The problem appears well motivated (sparse training can lead to significant power savings at the edge) and challenging (directly adding sparse training to conventional aggregation methods like FedAvg leads to significant accuracy drops).

2. The proposed solutions are good initial approaches backed by the empirical study and do appear to lead to some improvement in accuracy over the naive approach.

Weaknesses:

1. For a paper that relies entirely on empirical evidence for its claims, the empirical study appears a bit limited as it considers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and a single model architecture (ResNet18). Without seeing results for some other image/text datasets and other model architectures (MLP/LSTM) it is difficult to completely believe the claims especially since the proposed solutions rely heavily on the claims that the positions of the top-K weights do not vary significantly across models and across rounds.

2. The experimental results are also a bit limited. In particular:

a) It is misleading to highlight both max accuracy and max comms savings values since the two correspond to different settings and thus cannot be achieved simultaneously. I would suggest removing the bold font for the max comms savings model and just reporting the comms savings for the different mask ratios. An interesting future direction would be to think of a metric that can combine the two since it is currently unclear if the communication saving (and local computation cost reduction) is significant enough to compensate for the drop in accuracy w.r.t conventional FL (black line in Fig. 1)

b) Since the authors acknowledge that accuracy is expected to first increase and then decrease on increasing mask ratio, it would be worth including results for all mask ratios in the appendix at least to see the trend clearly.

c) The results with i.i.d data partitioning are not presented. Since from Fig 1 it seems like accuracy drops even for the i.i.d case, it is important to show if the proposed schemes alleviate the issue in that case as well.

d) Lastly, following point 1, in my opinion all experiments should be performed with some other datasets and model architectures before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

3. (Minor) There doesn't seem to be any real difference between Top-K-Weights and the Diff on Top-K-weights in that the weight updates will be exactly the same in both cases as far as I can tell. If that is so then there are essentially 2 (and not 3) new schemes being proposed.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The authors introduce an interesting and important problem but given that the paper relies purely on empirical evidence for its claims the current evaluation is quite limited and not enough for acceptance. I find the claims fairly intuitive, however, and so would be willing to increase my score if more empirical evidence (in line with my comments above) is presented to back them.

Comments after rebutal : I appreciate the added experiments which certainly demonstrate that the proposed schemes are able to improve performance across different datasets. I have increased my score to reflect the same.
reviews.correctness: 2: Several of the paper’s claims are incorrect or not well-supported.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies the problem of adding sparsity to local training in federated learning. Under the approach each node uses only the top-K weights for computations in the forward and backward pass of training (and only top-K activations for computations in the backward pass). The authors empirically show that adding sparsity in this way, while speeding up local training, significantly degrades the accuracy when directly used in federated learning. To mitigate this issue the authors propose to combine sparse training with sparse aggregation since their empirical study suggests that the accuracy drop may be due to dilution of weights that are active in only a few nodes. Experiments on CIFAR10 with non-IID splits using ResNet-18 as the model show some gain in accuracy along with a drop in communication cost when using the proposed schemes.

Main Review:
Strengths:

1. The authors introduce a new problem of adding sparse local training to federated learning. The problem appears well motivated (sparse training can lead to significant power savings at the edge) and challenging (directly adding sparse training to conventional aggregation methods like FedAvg leads to significant accuracy drops).

2. The proposed solutions are good initial approaches backed by the empirical study and do appear to lead to some improvement in accuracy over the naive approach.

Weaknesses:

1. For a paper that relies entirely on empirical evidence for its claims, the empirical study appears a bit limited as it considers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and a single model architecture (ResNet18). Without seeing results for some other image/text datasets and other model architectures (MLP/LSTM) it is difficult to completely believe the claims especially since the proposed solutions rely heavily on the claims that the positions of the top-K weights do not vary significantly across models and across rounds.

2. The experimental results are also a bit limited. In particular:

a) It is misleading to highlight both max accuracy and max comms savings values since the two correspond to different settings and thus cannot be achieved simultaneously. I would suggest removing the bold font for the max comms savings model and just reporting the comms savings for the different mask ratios. An interesting future direction would be to think of a metric that can combine the two since it is currently unclear if the communication saving (and local computation cost reduction) is significant enough to compensate for the drop in accuracy w.r.t conventional FL (black line in Fig. 1)

b) Since the authors acknowledge that accuracy is expected to first increase and then decrease on increasing mask ratio, it would be worth including results for all mask ratios in the appendix at least to see the trend clearly.

c) The results with i.i.d data partitioning are not presented. Since from Fig 1 it seems like accuracy drops even for the i.i.d case, it is important to show if the proposed schemes alleviate the issue in that case as well.

d) Lastly, following point 1, in my opinion all experiments should be performed with some other datasets and model architectures before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

3. (Minor) There doesn't seem to be any real difference between Top-K-Weights and the Diff on Top-K-weights in that the weight updates will be exactly the same in both cases as far as I can tell. If that is so then there are essentially 2 (and not 3) new schemes being proposed.

Summary Of The Review:
The authors introduce an interesting and important problem but given that the paper relies purely on empirical evidence for its claims the current evaluation is quite limited and not enough for acceptance. I find the claims fairly intuitive, however, and so would be willing to increase my score if more empirical evidence (in line with my comments above) is presented to back them.

Comments after rebutal : I appreciate the added experiments which certainly demonstrate that the proposed schemes are able to improve performance across different datasets. I have increased my score to reflect the same.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This work examines how imposing sparsity during local model updates in federated learning affects performance. Specifically, each client keeps only its top-K weights active in both forward and backward passes (and only its top-K activations during backpropagation). The authors first demonstrate that naively applying these sparse updates accelerates on-device training but at the cost of a substantial accuracy drop when using standard aggregation. To address this, they propose coupling sparse local training with a sparse aggregation mechanism—motivated by observations that parameters updated by only a few clients get “diluted” in a dense averaging step. Their experiments on CIFAR-10 under non-IID splits, using ResNet-18, show that the combined sparse training and sparse aggregation approach recovers much of the lost accuracy while also reducing communication overhead.

Strengths:  
1. Introducing the question of integrating sparse local training into federated learning is both timely and well argued—the potential power savings at edge devices are significant, and it is nontrivial to blend sparsity with standard FedAvg without hurting accuracy.  
2. The two proposed strategies are sensible first attempts, and the empirical results indicate they indeed outperform the naive sparse-only approach in terms of accuracy.

Weaknesses:  
1. Scope of Empirical Evaluation: Since the paper’s contributions rest entirely on empirical findings, the current evaluation feels narrow. All results are on a single dataset (CIFAR-10) and with one model family (ResNet-18). Given that the proposed fixes hinge on the assumption that top-K weight patterns are stable across rounds and architectures, we need evidence on other vision or language datasets (or on MLPs/LSTMs) to have confidence in the generality of these claims.  
2. Limitations in Presented Results:  
   a. Reporting both the highest accuracy and the maximum communication savings in bold is misleading, as those figures come from different experimental settings and are not achievable at once. It would be clearer to unbold the communication-saving numbers and simply list the savings for each mask ratio. In the long run, a unified metric that balances accuracy and communication/computation costs would be very helpful.  
   b. Since accuracy first rises and then falls as the mask ratio increases, it would be useful to include full curves for all mask ratios (at least in an appendix) to illustrate this trend clearly.  
   c. The paper omits any results under IID data partitions. Figure 1 hints that even in the IID case accuracy suffers; we need to see whether the proposed sparse aggregation remedies this drop under IID splits as well.  
   d. Echoing point 1, before this work can be accepted, it should validate its findings across additional datasets and network types.  
3. Minor Concern: Top-K-Weights and Diff-on-Top-K-Weights appear to produce identical weight updates, so it seems there are really only two distinct schemes rather than three.

Overall Recommendation:  
The authors tackle an important and relevant question and offer promising methods. However, because the paper is purely empirical, the evaluation must be broader to substantiate the claims. I find the ideas intuitive and would be happy to raise my recommendation if the authors extend their study as suggested.

Post-Rebuttal Comment:  
I appreciate the authors’ efforts to run extra experiments on additional datasets; these results strengthen the case that the proposed sparse aggregation schemes consistently improve performance. Accordingly, I have raised my score.

Rating: 6 – marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper investigates sparse training to accelerate on-device federated learning. It begins by taking an off-the-shelf sparse training method (SWAT) and analyzes its limitations. Then, the method proposes three strategies to improve upon SWAT. Experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10 dataset and show some improvements compared to SWAT.
reviews.main_review:
The paper has following strengths:
1.	Indeed, for more realistic deployment and to move federated learning towards practical systems, it is important to accelerate the on-device training (and not just the inference which is mainly the current practice). So, the problem the authors have considered is important.
2.	The proposed strategies clearly indicate the improvements over existing centralized sparse training technique like SWAT.
3.	Some of the insights shared (like the non-sparse weights staying the same throughout FL) were interesting.

The paper has following weaknesses:
1.	The main weakness of this paper is the experiments section. The results are presented only on CIFAR-10 dataset and do not consider many other datasets from Federated learning benchmarks (e.g., LEAF https://leaf.cmu.edu/). The authors should see relevant works like (FedProx https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and (FedMAX, https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657 (ECML 2020)) for details on different datasets and model types. If the experimental evaluation was comprehensive enough, this would be a very good paper (given the interesting and important problem it addresses).
2.	One other thing (although this is not the main focus of this paper), the authors should provide comparisons between strategies that result in fast convergence (without sparsity) vs. sparse methods? For example, do non-sparse, fast convergence methods (like FedProx, FedMAX, and others) result in small enough number of epochs compared to sparse methods? Can the fast convergence methods be augmented with sparisity ideas successfully without resulting in significant loss of accuracy? Some discussion and possibly performance numbers are needed here.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Even though the reviewer is quite happy about the problem addressed in this paper, it lacks a solid experimental section. I would be willing to increase the score if the authors can present a more comprehensive results section.

Post rebuttal:
I have read the author response and other reviews. Thanks to the authors for a detailed rebuttal and new experiments. It addressed my concerns and so I have increased the rating from 5 to 6.

reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript explores applying sparse training to speed up on-device federated learning. It starts by taking the off-the-shelf SWAT sparse training algorithm, examines its shortcomings in the federated setting, and then introduces three enhancements designed to overcome those limitations. The authors evaluate their approach on CIFAR-10 and report gains over the baseline SWAT method.

Strengths  
1.  The work tackles an underexplored but practically vital challenge: reducing the cost of on-device model training in federated learning rather than focusing solely on inference.  
2.  The three proposed strategies demonstrate clear benefits compared to the centralized sparse training approach exemplified by SWAT.  
3.  Some empirical observations—for instance, that the non-sparse weights remain essentially unchanged across FL rounds—offer useful insight.

Weaknesses  
1.  The experimental evaluation is too narrow. All results are confined to CIFAR-10, with no experiments on additional federated-learning benchmarks such as those in the LEAF suite (https://leaf.cmu.edu/). Prior FL papers like FedProx (https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and FedMAX (https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657) explore multiple datasets and model architectures; a similarly broad evaluation here would strengthen the paper considerably.  
2.  Although secondary to the core focus, it would be valuable to compare the proposed sparse-training enhancements against non-sparse methods optimized for rapid convergence (e.g., FedProx, FedMAX). Do these fast-converging dense approaches reach acceptable accuracy in fewer epochs than sparse training? And if so, can they be combined with sparsity techniques without incurring significant accuracy loss? Some discussion or preliminary results on this front are needed.

Overall Assessment  
I find the problem addressed to be important and the proposed solutions to be promising, but the limited scope of the experimental study prevents me from fully endorsing the work. If the authors extend their evaluation to more datasets and include comparisons to fast-converging dense FL methods, I would be comfortable raising my score.

Post-Rebuttal  
I have reviewed the authors’ response and the additional experiments they provided. Their new results address my primary concerns, so I have adjusted my rating upwards.

Final Rating  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper investigates sparse training to accelerate on-device federated learning. It begins by taking an off-the-shelf sparse training method (SWAT) and analyzes its limitations. Then, the method proposes three strategies to improve upon SWAT. Experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10 dataset and show some improvements compared to SWAT.

Main Review:
The paper has following strengths:
1.	Indeed, for more realistic deployment and to move federated learning towards practical systems, it is important to accelerate the on-device training (and not just the inference which is mainly the current practice). So, the problem the authors have considered is important.
2.	The proposed strategies clearly indicate the improvements over existing centralized sparse training technique like SWAT.
3.	Some of the insights shared (like the non-sparse weights staying the same throughout FL) were interesting.

The paper has following weaknesses:
1.	The main weakness of this paper is the experiments section. The results are presented only on CIFAR-10 dataset and do not consider many other datasets from Federated learning benchmarks (e.g., LEAF https://leaf.cmu.edu/). The authors should see relevant works like (FedProx https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and (FedMAX, https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657 (ECML 2020)) for details on different datasets and model types. If the experimental evaluation was comprehensive enough, this would be a very good paper (given the interesting and important problem it addresses).
2.	One other thing (although this is not the main focus of this paper), the authors should provide comparisons between strategies that result in fast convergence (without sparsity) vs. sparse methods? For example, do non-sparse, fast convergence methods (like FedProx, FedMAX, and others) result in small enough number of epochs compared to sparse methods? Can the fast convergence methods be augmented with sparisity ideas successfully without resulting in significant loss of accuracy? Some discussion and possibly performance numbers are needed here.

Summary Of The Review:
Even though the reviewer is quite happy about the problem addressed in this paper, it lacks a solid experimental section. I would be willing to increase the score if the authors can present a more comprehensive results section.

Post rebuttal:
I have read the author response and other reviews. Thanks to the authors for a detailed rebuttal and new experiments. It addressed my concerns and so I have increased the rating from 5 to 6.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.
reviews.main_review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.

Main Review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
The authors introduce ZeroFL, a federated learning approach that extends the SWAT sparse‐training framework to edge devices. In SWAT, a target sparsity level sp is enforced by keeping only the top-k weights during forward and backward passes, enabling the use of sparse convolutions and faster training. ZeroFL has each client train with sparsity sp but, when uploading updates, they send a fraction 1 – sp + r_mask of weights instead of just 1 – sp. This “slack” r_mask is justified by an ablation showing vanilla SWAT performs poorly in federated averaging, and the extra weights help deliver a cleaner signal to the server. The paper also proposes three variants of server updates: (1) aggregating client top-k weights, (2) aggregating the difference between server and client top-k weights, and (3) taking top-k of those differences. Experiments are reported on CIFAR-10 with 100 clients under IID and non-IID splits.

Main Review  
This manuscript is clearly written and the method is straightforward to implement, which are definite pluses. However, I believe significant revisions are needed before this could be accepted:

1. Missing Baselines  
   - There is no comparison against federated dropout (at the weight level), which is an obvious alternative for reducing upload costs.  

2. Incremental Contribution  
   - ZeroFL largely follows SWAT’s approach and adds only a small adjustment (the r_mask slack).  
   - The authors note that SWAT also sparsifies activations, yet they do not include any activation‐sparsity experiments here.  

3. Experimental Scope  
   - Results are confined to a single dataset (CIFAR-10) with only 100 clients. It would strengthen the paper to evaluate on larger federated benchmarks (e.g., EMNIST/FEMNIST).  
   - All metrics appear to come from one random seed, making it impossible to assess result stability.  

4. Communication Overhead Details  
   - The main text omits any discussion of the cost of transmitting indices for unstructured sparsity, even though the table caption briefly mentions CSC format.  
   - The derivation of the “0.5 (r_mask – sp)” savings in Section 5 is unclear, and it’s not evident whether it accounts for the index transmission overhead.  

Additional Comments  
- Privacy Claim: The paper claims that its sparsity scheme better preserves privacy. This needs justification: allowing each client a unique mask could leak more information than a uniform server‐defined pattern.  
- Download Compression: While ZeroFL targets upload savings, have the authors considered compressing the server-to-clients updates as well?  
- Algorithm Presentation: The pseudocode would benefit from explicit if-conditions to distinguish the three update rules. Also, Figure 3’s color palette and line styles make it hard to read; stronger contrast would help.  
- Table 1 Caption: The 7.4× speedup is reported “over vanilla SWAT,” but the numbers seem to compare against a dense model. Please clarify the baseline.  
- Notation Consistency: The paper alternates between “top-K” and “top-K%.” Choosing one term and using it consistently will avoid confusion.  

Overall Assessment  
ZeroFL is a direct, modest extension of SWAT to the federated setting, with only a minor tweak (the r_mask slack). The empirical evaluation is limited to one dataset and omits key baselines, making it difficult to gauge the method’s practical value. For these reasons, I recommend rejection at this time.

Rating  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Complex Locomotion Skill Learning via Differentiable Physics

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes to learn locomotion skills on soft robots made of springs. It proposes to leverage differentiable physics along with a NN controller based on SIREN, which allows to directly learn policies by minimizing loss functions defined on trajectories. The methods allows to learn locomotion and jump behaviors on a various set of 2D and 3D mass-spring systems.
reviews.main_review:
## Strengths

* The loss function design is clear and well explained, it is specifically tailored for cyclic motion which is the main task addressed by the paper.
* The use of SIREN to process the input and output a periodical signal is quite interesting. It suits well to tasks requiring cyclic motion.
* The comparison to PPO seems fair and thorough, I appreciate that the authors put efforts into having a well-tuned baseline, something which is often overlooked.
* The control of complex mass spring system is impressive

## Weaknesses

* The claim: "To the best of our knowledge, we proposed the first differentiable physics based learning frame-
work for complex locomotion tasks using single network." is a bit fishy.
Many RL algorithms handle locomotion tasks of humanoid-like robots in simulation with a single neural network. I understand the current framework use differentiable physics for mass-spring systems, and thus is not directly applicable to classical RL benchmarks without a differentiable physics simulator. Such a claim could be made if it solved classical RL locomotion tasks + harder ones for example.

* The experiment section is not really convincing and hard to follow. 
** Table 1 is barely commented, there is only a bold number in one column. Only tanh activation function is compared to sin, while relu, gelu, sigmoid could be used for hidden layers with an activation function outputting a value in [-1, 1] for the final layer of the network. Given that using SIREN seems to be an important component, the paper would benefit from a better ablation study. 
** The importance of features section is really unclear. 
There are sentences like "The results show that each of the three parts serves as a necessary
component for a converged training. The periodic control signal serves as an important role. The
agent can hardly move without this signal" without reference to a table or a figure, so I do not know what to look to confirm the statement.
Figure 6 is also hard to read and I am not sure if the section is super insightful. To my understanding it validates the design choice of the input feature which is a bit minor compared to other components of the model.
* In the abstract, a modified Adam optimizer is mentioned "we find our adoption of batching and modified Adam optimizer effective in training complex locomotion tasks". It seems to be important according to the authors but there is no experiment on vanilla Adam vs the proposed one with reduced momentum so we cannot asses how useful it is.

## Other comments

* The abbreviations of Table 1 could be put as caption rather than footnote for readability. The back-and-forth between table and footnote is not great for the reader.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is interesting, proposing a clean framework to perform locomotion tasks on mass-spring systems. The benefit of using differentiable physics is clearly highlighted and the use of SIREN to learn periodic motions is on point. However the experimental section is weak. For this reason I only grade it as weak-reject but would be inclined to bump it if this part is strengthened.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Considering the periodic nature of locomotion, the author proposed to use sine activation layers and periodic signals in different phases as input. The experiments show that a larger batch size can lead to faster convergence. Compared to SGD, this paper points out that Adam is better for the tasks. The supplementary video presents that the trained networks enable real-time control of complex skills.
reviews.main_review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.
3. In Figure 4, what is the definition of one iteration? Is it one step of network update or one step of simulation?

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript delivers a hands-on recipe for training neural‐network controllers for locomotion tasks in a robust and efficient manner. The authors break down target behaviors—running, jumping, crawling—into discrete motion primitives, each governed by a bespoke objective function. Exploiting the inherently periodic nature of locomotion, they incorporate sine‐based activation layers and cyclical phase signals as network inputs. Empirical findings indicate that larger batch sizes speed up convergence and that Adam outperforms SGD for these tasks. A supplementary video demonstrates that the learned policies achieve real-time control of complex movements.

Detailed Review  
Building on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper supplies a set of practical guidelines for leveraging physics‐based gradients to construct capable controllers. Such a distilled collection of “recipes” can serve as a valuable reference for researchers adopting differentiable simulation in control problems.

The approach segments long‐horizon locomotion into shorter periodic intervals, each optimized by its own objective to sidestep sparse or jagged reward landscapes. To drive the controller, the authors prepend a periodic control signal to the input and adopt sine activations within the network. During training, they employ mini‐batching, carefully chosen regularization terms, and these periodic activations to both accelerate and stabilize learning.

Questions and Concerns  
1. Equation 5: It is unclear how the velocity target g_v(n) is subtracted from the actuation A(t)—do they share the same dimensionality? g_v(n) as a velocity vector should be in ℝ³ (or ℝ⁶ including orientation), whereas A(t) seems to match the actuator DOFs. Perhaps you intended the norm ||g_v(n)||? Also, why only penalize velocity errors and not height deviations g_h(n)? Finally, could you clarify the statement that “periodic control signals may induce unwanted high‐frequency noise”?  
2. The manuscript references “a modified Adam optimizer,” yet there is no description of what modifications were applied to the standard Adam algorithm.  
3. In Figure 4, what precisely constitutes an “iteration”? Is this one network‐weight update, one simulation timestep, or something else?

Overall Assessment  
This work offers a clear example of how to harness differentiable physics engines to train locomotion controllers. The accompanying video showcasing interactive 2D and 3D robot control is compelling. Although the individual components—staged objectives, batching, sine activations, and periodic inputs—are familiar tricks, it is worthwhile to see their combination reliably yield performant controllers.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.

Main Review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
The authors explore the use of a differentiable physics simulator to train deformable characters in a variety of locomotion tasks. Their main contribution lies in a broad empirical study of design choices—such as activation functions, reward formulations, optimizers, and batch sizes—and how these factors affect learning. They demonstrate that, with the right combination of these settings, a single policy can acquire multiple locomotion behaviors and notably outperform a PPO-based baseline.

Main Review:

Strengths:  
1) The work successfully marries a differentiable simulation framework with carefully selected design parameters, resulting in effective soft‐body locomotion learning.  
2) The thorough ablation study and analysis offer concrete, practical guidance for researchers and practitioners working on reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weaknesses:  
1) The technical novelty is limited. The Taichi differentiable simulator used here is existing technology, and the paper appears to adopt it with few modifications. The second claimed contribution—an end-to-end differentiable environment incorporating mass-spring and material point methods—is described only at a high level (mostly in Section 2), leaving its actual scope and innovations unclear.  
2) The experimental showcase could be more compelling and varied. Tasks like navigating uneven terrain or soft-robot object manipulation would strengthen the paper. Demonstrating a soft character running on flat ground has precedent (e.g., DiffTaichi). Although merging multiple skills (turning, speed variation, jumping) in one policy is useful, the advancement over prior work feels incremental.

Additional Questions and Comments:  
1) In Section 2.1, the authors state that “any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework.” Yet, it seems differentiability is a strict requirement. Am I overlooking something?  
2) It’s not clear how broadly the ablation results apply beyond locomotion. For example, sine‐based actuation and periodic signals may not prove as beneficial in manipulation scenarios. If the scope truly is limited to locomotion, the paper’s contributions feel more narrowly focused.

Overall Assessment:  
This manuscript delivers solid results in soft‐body locomotion and provides a valuable breakdown of how design choices influence performance. While the degree of technical innovation is modest, the practical insights from the ablation studies are likely to benefit the community. On balance, I lean slightly toward accepting this work.

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.
reviews.main_review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.

Main Review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows interesting results of locomotion tasks, but the overall technical contribution is limited as presented. The writing of the paper could also be further improved. I am willing to raise my score if the listed concerns can be addressed.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:
This work focuses on training neural network controllers for soft robot locomotion within a fully differentiable simulator. The authors introduce several technical refinements—namely, periodic activation functions, tailored loss formulations, and large minibatch training—to improve controller robustness and sample efficiency. They demonstrate the approach on soft robots performing running, jumping, and crawling behaviors, with the ability to switch goals interactively.

Strength:
* +++ The interactive manipulation of soft-robot locomotion and seamless switching between objectives is impressively demonstrated.

Weaknesses:
* --- The paper’s core technical novelty appears modest. The proposed loss formulations are specifically tailored to the locomotion benchmarks, and it remains unclear if they extend to other robotic platforms or real-world scenarios [1, 2].
* -- The claimed benefits of using a sine activation are not convincingly backed by the presented experiments. In Table 1, the sine nonlinearity yields only a slight edge over tanh, while Table 5 in the appendix even shows tanh outperforming sine. Moreover, tanh is known to saturate over long backpropagation chains. A comparison against ReLU activations would strengthen the argument.
* -- Several aspects of the paper’s presentation could be clarified:
     1. The “modified Adam optimizer” is highlighted as a contribution, yet the specific modifications and their impact relative to vanilla Adam are not described or evaluated.
     2. Robot names first appear in Figure 7 (Section 4.5), but they are referred to earlier in the text and in Table 1. Introducing and defining the agent names upfront would improve readability.
     3. The citation to Figure 5 at the end of Section 4.2 seems erroneous, as that figure does not include the SGD-versus-Adam comparison it claims.

References:
[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. “Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain.” Science Robotics 5.47 (2020).  
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. “RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design.” ACM TOG 39.6 (2020): 1–16.

Overall Assessment:
The paper delivers compelling demonstrations of learned soft-robot locomotion and interactive control, but the technical contributions are somewhat narrow in scope, and key experimental and presentation details require clarification. Addressing the concerns above could substantially improve the work, and I would reconsider my score should these issues be resolved.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: On the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Feature Propagation in Learning on Graphs with Missing Node Features

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Initial assessment : accept

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. Missing value imputation is a problem that is often ill-addressed when it comes to ML research. In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice. 

other comments:
P1 last paragraph : I’m not sure if being unaware to the task is a bad thing necessarily.  isn't it better to do imputation in a task agnostic fashion?

Fig 3: Please make the text larger. The figures are hard to read. 

Fig 3: Also any intuition why Random is so much worse than Zero in all these datasets?

Summary Of The Paper:
The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.
