Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?

Summary Of The Review:
Initial assessment : accept

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. Missing value imputation is a problem that is often ill-addressed when it comes to ML research. In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice. 

other comments:
P1 last paragraph : I’m not sure if being unaware to the task is a bad thing necessarily.  isn't it better to do imputation in a task agnostic fashion?

Fig 3: Please make the text larger. The figures are hard to read. 

Fig 3: Also any intuition why Random is so much worse than Zero in all these datasets?

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This work tackles the problem of training graph neural networks when node features are partially missing. The authors introduce a diffusion-inspired preprocessing step that, when discretized, becomes a scalable “feature propagation” algorithm for imputing missing attributes. They posit that an underlying energy function governing feature correlations can be learned directly from the data. By framing the imputation as a gradient flow minimizing Dirichlet energy, they avoid the expense of computing a closed‐form solution. Empirical results on several benchmarks demonstrate that this approach outperforms a range of baselines in both accuracy and runtime.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Presentation: The manuscript is well structured and the narrative is easy to follow.  
2. Novelty and Elegance: The central idea—a simple diffusion‐based imputation preceding GNN training—is both intuitive and elegant.  
3. Thorough Experiments: The evaluation covers not only predictive performance but also computational efficiency, showing clear advantages over competing methods.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Extension to Vector‐Valued Features  
   • It remains unclear how the method generalizes when each node has a multi‐dimensional feature vector.  
   • Do the authors apply the diffusion process independently to each feature dimension, or is there a joint propagation that exploits correlations among dimensions?  
   • Clarifying this point and discussing its impact on computational cost would strengthen the paper.  

2. Comparison with Label Propagation  
   • The authors argue that their method differs from traditional label propagation because labels are discrete and the diffusion operator here is distinct.  
   • However, it is not evident why this distinction fundamentally changes the algorithmic structure—would it not simply amount to swapping in a different operator?  
   • A more detailed discussion contrasting the two methods and highlighting any practical or theoretical implications would be helpful.

Minor Comments  
• Page 1, last paragraph: The authors note that their imputation is task‐agnostic and “unaware of the downstream objective.” I am not convinced this is a drawback—task‐independent imputations can be more versatile across applications. A brief remark defending this choice could preempt reader concerns.  
• Figure 3: The axis labels and legend text are rather small; increasing their font size would improve readability.  
• Figure 3 (continued): It is surprising that the Random baseline performs so much worse than Zero imputation across all datasets. Any intuition or hypothesis for this pattern would be a valuable addition.

Decision: 8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: A graph neural network (GNN) pre-processing step for completing missing node features is presented: feature propagation (FP). The method is based on minimisation of Dirichlet energy. A more efficient iterative update aproach is derived avoiding matrix inversion (cubic operation) to a series of sparse-to-dense matrix multiplications. The method acts similar to a low-pass filter for missing features, which complements the GNN learning algorithm. Relevant work is covered and similarities and differences mentioned in many cases. Experiments on seven publicly available datasets show that the method is effective and performs especially well when a large proportion of the feature values are missing (above 90%). A run-time analysis of wall-clock performance compares their method to two state-of-the-art methods for handling missing node features in GNNs and their method is 3 x faster in these comparison. A weakness of their method, FP, is analysed; FP assumes the graphs are homophlic, that nearby nodes have similar node features but if this is not the case then the method does not perform well. This is some interesting future work.
reviews.main_review:
This paper addresses a very interesting problem that is relevant to real-world applications; dealing with missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach is based on minimising Dirichlet energy, which is a emthod previously used for graph regularisation and has relations with graph Laplcians, so is well suited to working with data on graph topology. The author(s) derive an efficient iterative algorithm which makes the approach scalable to large graphs which makes it more practical for real-world applications. Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results.

In the experiments, it's surprising how well the nearest-neigbour averaging worked. In the comparison method's own papers SAT paper has a 'NeighAggre' baseline that seems to do something similar but gets worse scores than their method and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5, and this also performs worse than their method. Do you have some insights into why neighbourhood averaging performs so well?

This method is a pre-processing step, not integrated into the GNN, as such many other methods for estimating the missing values could be used. One justification of this choice of approach is that is acts as a low-pass filter, and the GNN mainly learns from the lower frequency components.  This work is very close to matrix completion with graph side information.In this domain there are other methods that are highly scalable and are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of the data (>99%) are missing [1, 2, 4]. Furthermore, one method uses a similar optimisation approach of iterative dense-sparse matrix mulitplication [1]. Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
As this is a preprocessing step that essentially is similar to matrix completion with graph side information, then I believe that comparison with this literature is missing. Otherwise this is a very good paper. The approach they have for the problem, which could be seen as matrix completion with graph side information, is I believe novel in that domain, as well as in this framework for graph neural networks. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
The authors introduce a preprocessing technique—Feature Propagation (FP)—to impute missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). FP is derived by minimizing Dirichlet energy, yielding an update rule that replaces a costly matrix inversion with repeated sparse–dense matrix multiplications. Functionally, FP behaves like a low-pass filter on the feature signals, complementing downstream GNN training. The paper reviews related work thoroughly, highlighting both overlaps and distinctions. Empirical results on seven public benchmarks indicate FP excels when over 90 % of feature entries are missing. A wall-clock runtime study shows FP is roughly three times faster than two leading alternatives. The authors also acknowledge FP’s reliance on homophily—when node features are not smooth over the graph, performance degrades—pointing to this as a direction for future work.

Main Review  
This manuscript tackles an important and practical challenge: how to handle missing node attributes in GNNs. The choice to frame the problem as Dirichlet energy minimization is well motivated, given its roots in graph regularization and its connection to graph Laplacians. The derivation of an iterative solver that scales to large, sparse graphs is a strong point, making FP appealing for real-world datasets. The experimental section employs a variety of publicly available graphs, and the results are encouraging.

One surprising finding is how well a simple nearest-neighbour averaging baseline performs. Both the SAT paper’s “NeighAggre” and GCNMF with k = 5 underperform compared to FP, yet they implement a similar idea. Can the authors shed light on why this neighborhood averaging is so effective in their experiments?

Because FP is applied as a standalone preprocessing step, it’s inherently modular: other imputation algorithms could be slotted in. The authors argue this particular choice serves as a low-pass filter aligned with GNNs’ tendency to exploit low-frequency signals. However, this approach closely mirrors matrix completion methods that leverage graph side information. In that broader literature, there exist highly scalable techniques capable of imputing over 99 % missing data [1,2,4], and at least one employs an analogous iterative sparse–dense multiplication [1]. An extension of [1] even prunes edges based on homophily, which could mitigate FP’s weakness on low-homophily graphs. Additionally, [3] offers a kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization framework with diffusion kernels, akin to FP’s smoothing. I recommend at minimum discussing these methods and, ideally, including one or two in the experimental comparison—particularly [1], given its similar computational strategy.

Minor Comments  
• Please specify the GPU configuration (V100 with 16 GB or 32 GB?) and report how much memory GCNMF consumed before OOM occurs.  
• For vector-valued node features, do you run FP independently per feature dimension, or is there a joint strategy?  
• Proposition 1 assumes a connected graph because the Laplacian inversion is undefined when eigenvalues vanish. How common are disconnected components in real datasets, and how would FP handle them?  
• How was the choice of 40 iterations determined? Does further iteration risk over-smoothing, causing all imputed features to converge to the same value?  
• You state, “For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments.” Does this imply the two-layer GCN downstream model also uses identical hyperparameters in every experiment?  

Typos/Edits  
• In several equations “A_u” should read “A_{uu}.”  
• Complete the Zhu and Ghahramani reference.  
• Fix “donwstream task” → “downstream task.”

Relevant References  
1. Rao, N., et al. “Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods.” NIPS 2015.  
2. Strahl, J., et al. “Scalable Probabilistic Matrix Factorization with Graph-Based Priors.” AAAI 2020.  
3. Zhou, T., et al. “Kernelized Probabilistic Matrix Factorization: Exploiting Graphs and Side Information.” SIAM ICDM 2012.  
4. Monti, F., Bronstein, M. M., and Bresson, X. “Geometric Matrix Completion with Recurrent Multi-Graph Neural Networks.” arXiv 2017.

Overall Assessment  
FP is a well-executed preprocessing method that effectively imputes missing node attributes and integrates neatly with GNN pipelines. However, because FP aligns closely with graph-based matrix completion techniques, the manuscript needs to position itself more explicitly within that body of work and empirically compare to the most relevant scalable methods (especially [1]). Without this, the claim of novelty feels limited to framing rather than substance. After revisions, the paper could become quite strong, but in its current form it insufficiently engages with closely related matrix completion approaches.

Decision  
Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.
reviews.main_review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:

Main Review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.

Summary Of The Review:
Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
The authors introduce a computationally efficient method for imputing missing node features in graphs. Their core idea is to incorporate the graph’s structure by minimizing, feature by feature, the Laplacian quadratic form (Dirichlet energy). Since solving this directly requires inverting a (potentially large) matrix, they instead apply a gradient descent scheme built on sparse matrix–vector operations.

Detailed Comments:  
1. Empirical Validation:  
   - The experiments convincingly demonstrate that this straightforward strategy improves imputation quality over several baselines.

2. Concerns about Novelty:  
   - The use of the Laplacian’s quadratic form for propagating labels or interpolating graph signals is by now a well-trodden path, with numerous applications over the past ten years (many of which are cited here).  
   - Likewise, replacing direct Laplacian inversion with iterative gradient-type updates is also a familiar technique. For instance, the tutorial “Signal processing on higher‐order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond” (Section 2.4.2) explicitly describes how repeated application of (I–L)^k amounts to k gradient‐descent steps on the Dirichlet energy. Equation (4) in that reference is essentially the same construct.  
   - From the vantage of graph signal processing, the exact imputation via the Laplacian inverse is viewed as an infinite‐impulse‐response (IIR) filter, whereas the proposed gradient approach is just a finite‐impulse‐response (FIR) approximation. The broader topic of designing such graph filters has seen extensive study in the last seven years.

Overall Assessment:  
While the paper delivers a neat, efficient implementation and backs it up with solid experiments, the foundational ideas—Dirichlet energy minimization and its gradient‐based approximation—have been explored repeatedly in prior work. As a result, the contribution in terms of novelty is quite limited.

Rating:  
5 – Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.

Main Review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.

Summary Of The Review:
Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper address the problem of  missing node features in graph by conducting feature propagation (FP). An iterative updated algorithm is presented. FP is a preprocessing step and can be used in multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on node classification shows the effectiveness of FP. The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.
reviews.main_review:
The strengths:

1. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow
2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

1. FP is not a new idea/algorithm, it is essentially same as label propagation (LP) by Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argued that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator.", from my understanding, there is not substantial difference. LP also propagate the label probability, which is continuous. The derivation, the update formula of FP are the same as LP, the only difference is to substitute the weight matrix in LP with Laplacian matrix. Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. Consider $x_i$'s as the soft label with label function $f_i$, then they are essentially the same thing.
2. The experiment part shows that simple averaging of the neighbors gives good result when the missing portion is $\leq$ 50%. In that case, I think it is also important to show experimental results of algorithms that do not use node features, to see if there is really a gap on the performance. (Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. If the performance is similar as what FP gives when there are 99% features missing, I would argue that in that case we can simply go with classical algorithms using only the network topology. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

[2] Solomon, Justin, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. "Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning." In International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 306-314. PMLR, 2014.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: In general I like the idea of using FP as a preprocessing step. However, I am not convinced that the idea is novel enough to be accepted in ICLR, details are referred to my main review.

reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This manuscript investigates the challenge of missing node features in graphs by introducing a feature propagation (FP) method. The authors develop an iterative update algorithm that serves as a preprocessing step and can be applied to various downstream tasks. They evaluate FP on node classification benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness under extreme feature absence, and also provide runtime comparisons as well as an analysis of how graph homophily impacts FP’s performance.

Main Review:

Strengths:  
1. The writing is clear and the presentation flows logically.  
2. The results are striking—FP maintains strong classification accuracy even when 99% of node features are missing.  
3. The reported speedups over baselines are encouraging, indicating that FP is computationally efficient.

Weaknesses:  
1. Novelty Concerns: The proposed FP approach appears to be fundamentally the same as the label propagation (LP) algorithm of Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argue that their diffusion operates on continuous feature vectors (as opposed to directly diffusing discrete class labels), LP already propagates soft label probabilities, making the core update equations effectively identical. The only apparent change is replacing LP’s affinity matrix with a graph Laplacian. Moreover, the reinterpretation of propagation via Dirichlet energy was previously explored by Solomon et al. [2], who use label functions f_i and f_j in place of x_i and x_j. If we treat the x_i values in this work as soft labels, the two frameworks coincide, calling into question the claimed methodological novelty.  
2. Experimental Comparisons: The experiments reveal that simply averaging neighbor features yields strong performance when up to 50% of features are missing. To validate FP’s added value, it is crucial to compare against methods that rely solely on topology. Under homophily, neighbor averaging already performs well, and FP (like LP) extends this to a weighted global average. Other graph-based approaches—such as classic label propagation on node classes or representation learning via LINE, DeepWalk, or node2vec followed by a downstream classifier—may achieve comparable results, especially in the 99% missing feature regime. If such topology-only techniques match FP’s performance, one might prefer these established algorithms rather than adopting a GNN-based pipeline. As it stands, the paper does not convincingly demonstrate that FP outperforms or meaningfully differs from these baselines when node features are nearly entirely absent.

References:  
[1] Xiaojin Zhu and Zoubin Ghahramani. “Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation.” CMU Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, 2002.  
[2] Justin Solomon, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. “Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning.” In ICML, pp. 306–314, 2014.

Summary of the Review:  
I appreciate the clear exposition and the practical appeal of using FP as a preprocessing step. Nonetheless, I remain unconvinced that the approach is sufficiently novel relative to classical label propagation methods or that the experimental evidence rules out simpler topology-only baselines. Consequently, I do not recommend accepting this paper in its current form.

Rating:  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Learning Audio-Visual Dereverberation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.

Main Review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript introduces an audio-visual dereverberation framework in which speech enhancement is conditioned on RGB and depth imagery (either standard field-of-view or panoramic). A new dataset is constructed from real 3D home scans populated with Librispeech utterances, and real-world recordings via loudspeaker playback (providing clean references) are also included. The core architecture is a U-Net that takes visual-acoustic embeddings and directly predicts spectrograms. Performance is assessed with PESQ, speech-recognition WER, and speaker-verification EER on both synthetic and real data. The audio-visual version yields only slight gains over an audio-only variant, which itself outperforms several literature baselines on synthetic data but shows mixed results on real recordings.

Main Review  

Strengths  
• First audio-visual dereverberation: To my knowledge, no prior work has explored visual cues for speech dereverberation. It’s compelling to gauge how much spatial and geometric information can aid reverberation removal.  
• Valuable dataset: Most synthetic reverberation uses shoebox simulators; having realistic, scan-based environments is a significant advance. Including real recordings with loudspeaker playback (so that clean targets exist) is particularly useful, and I hope the authors will publish their capture protocol.  
• Comprehensive ablations: The paper not only studies the audio-visual system but also evaluates the audio-only backbone and component losses (e.g. the matching loss), which I appreciate.  
• Clarity and organization: The manuscript is well structured and clearly written.

Weaknesses  
W1) Artifacts in audio demos  
– Listening to the supplemental samples, I noted a “buzziness” under moderate reverberation and a “twanginess” at higher RT60s. Objective metrics like PESQ may not fully capture these perceptual artifacts. A human listening test (e.g. MUSHRA) would provide stronger validation. Consider alternative loss functions—L1 on spectrogram magnitude or MSE on compressed spectrograms—to better align with human perception.  

W2) Frame stitching strategy  
– The current pipeline chops audio into non-overlapping 2.56 s windows and concatenates outputs, which seems suboptimal. An overlap-add approach—using, for instance, a Hann window or time-domain inversion with overlap-add and end-to-end training—would likely yield smoother reconstructions.  

W3) Unclear origin of random speech embedding  
– The paper mentions a “random speech embedding sampled from the data batch.” Where does this vector originate? How is it computed, and is it meant to represent speaker identity?  

W4) Marginal visual gains  
– The benefit from adding visual input over the audio-only model is minimal. While the audio-only version surpasses several synthetic baselines, its PESQ on real data actually decreases relative to other methods. It would strengthen the work to benchmark against strong dereverberation systems (e.g. top entries from the REVERB Challenge). At present, I remain unconvinced that the visual stream provides substantive improvement.  

W5) Model capacity vs. performance  
– Table 1 should include the number of trainable parameters for each method. Is the proposed model’s edge simply due to larger capacity? Note that WPE has no learned parameters, since it’s an analytical EM-based algorithm.  

W6) Noise robustness and real-world applicability  
– Training and evaluation exclude noise, which limits deployment in realistic settings. I appreciate the authors’ WHAM! experiment, but 20 dB SNR is still relatively benign. It would be informative to report performance across a range of lower SNRs to gauge robustness.

Minor Comments  
M1) On the statement “R is a function of… the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener”: An RIR can depend on absolute source and receiver positions (e.g., proximity to walls), not just their relative geometry.  
M2) For real-world recordings, please specify the actual source-to-microphone distances rather than simply “near-field to mid-field to far-field.”  
M3) When you note “For each location, we play around 10 utterances,” please clarify the exact count, speaker genders, and whether speakers vary across rooms.  
M4) Consistency: use “U-Net” rather than “UNet.”  
M5) The claim “our model is agnostic to the audio source type” should be qualified, since it depends on the diversity of the training set.  
M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I suggest describing “clean speech (upper bound)” as “anechoic speech (upper bound)” to avoid the noise connotation of “clean.”  
M7) For the blind RT60/DRR/distance prediction errors (“3e-3 s, 160 dB and 17.92 m”), please state whether these are absolute or squared errors.

Overall Recommendation  
I recommend marginal acceptance (rating 6). This work tackles a novel direction—audio-visual dereverberation—and contributes a realistic synthetic and real dataset that will benefit the community. However, the added value of visual information over an audio-only approach is modest, and I have reservations about the perceptual quality of the outputs. Despite these concerns, the paper presents enough innovation and valuable resources to warrant publication.

Rating  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.

Main Review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents a deep neural net-based dereverberation algorithm that uses both audio and video modalities. Based on the observation that a visual scene captured by a camera conveys information that is related to room characteristics, the authors propose a visually informed audio dereverberation method that aims to extract clean, anechoic speech from reverberant speech. In doing so, they first construct a large audio-visual dataset synthesized using a 3D simulator for real-world scanned environments and LibriSpeech data. They then train deep neural networks that take as inputs both visual data (RGB and depth images) and audio data (reverberant speech), and output clean speech. When training, two types of losses - one for clean speech spectrogram estimation and the other for reverb-visual matching - are used. Through the experiments with several downstream tasks for speech, they showed that the proposed audio-visual dererberation method outperforms the baseline models, both for synthetic and real-world test data.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
- first multi-modal (audio-visual) approach applied to dereverberation
- paper is well organized and straightforward to follow
- solid experiments with ablation studies and in-depth analyses
- supplementary materials provide useful information not explained/described in the main text

Weaknesses:
- some of the key arguments are not sufficiently validated
- contributions are marginal and limited
- experiments and evaluation are not thorough

The key idea in this work is that visual input captured by a camera conveys information that characterizes room acoustics. With all the experiments and ablation studies, however, I don't believe that the authors provide sufficient information that support the aforementioned key hypothesis. The authors did take into account this matter in two ways: first, they included a reverb-visual matching loss term to penalize a matching between random speech and visual input, but little experiment or analysis was performed on this loss term - varying the weight or using different loss functions, for example. With or without using a reverb-visual matching loss was included in the ablation studies, but the effect was not significant compared with other methods. The performance was better with the audio-only model even in speech quality metric (PESQ). The second way is to use a random image instead of using the image that matches the room impulse response. In such scenario, the images does not carry any information relevant to the room acoustics, and thus the performance should be worse than or comparable to that of the audio-only method. However, the performance is better in general and is even best for some metric. Further experiments and deeper analysis are warranted to investigate this issue.

Another point that I hope to be added is the comparison of the model efficiency. Adding a visual embedding network increases the number of model parameters and computational costs. The proposed method is likely to work in real-time scenarios, and possibly on small devices, the efficiency should also be considered.

Lastly, I'd like to suggest to include R-vectors approaches as another baseline for comparison.

The idea is interesting and somewhat novel, but the experimental validation is not thorough enough to support the key idea of reverb-visual matching nor to provide sufficient evidences that visual stream does convey information about room acoustics.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript introduces a deep learning framework for speech dereverberation that incorporates both audio and video inputs. Motivated by the premise that visual imagery of a room encodes acoustic characteristics, the authors synthesize a large-scale audiovisual corpus—combining rendered 3D environments of real-world scans with LibriSpeech audio—and train neural networks to map reverberant speech and corresponding RGB-D frames to clean, anechoic speech. Training employs two loss terms: one focused on reconstructing the clean speech spectrogram and another designed to enforce consistency between reverberation characteristics and the visual scene. The authors demonstrate gains over audio-only and other baselines on several downstream speech tasks, using both simulated and real recordings.

Strengths  
- This is the first work, to my knowledge, to tackle dereverberation via a true audio-visual model.  
- The paper’s organization is clear, with a logical flow that makes it easy to follow.  
- The experimental section is fairly comprehensive, including ablation studies and detailed analyses.  
- The supplementary material adds valuable implementation details and additional results not in the main text.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Insufficient validation of the core hypothesis.  
   • The central claim is that visual frames carry room-acoustic cues. The ablation on the reverb–visual matching loss shows only marginal impact, and the authors do not explore variations of this loss (e.g., different weights or alternative formulations).  
   • In the “random image” experiment—where the visual input is decoupled from the actual room—the model still often outperforms audio-only, and in some metrics even achieves the best results. This counterintuitive finding casts doubt on whether the network truly leverages visual information. I recommend more controlled analyses (e.g., systematically varying loss weights, testing additional loss types) to ascertain the role of the visual stream.  
2. Limited assessment of computational efficiency.  
   Incorporating a visual embedding network inevitably increases parameters and runtime. If this approach is intended for real-time or on-device applications, the authors should report model size, inference speed, and possibly comparisons on resource-constrained hardware.  
3. Missing baseline comparison.  
   The paper would benefit from including R-vector (room embedding) methods as an additional point of reference in the experiments.

Overall Evaluation  
The idea of using images to inform speech dereverberation is intriguing and represents a novel direction, but the experimental evidence falls short of conclusively demonstrating that the visual modality contributes the hypothesized room-acoustic information. Further experimentation and efficiency analyses are needed to bolster the core claims.

Rating  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper describes an audio de-reverberation method which integrates (panoramic, rgb+depth map) visual input with a spectrogram U-Net to estimate a de-reverberated spectrogram and recover the original clean signal.
The proposed method is compared to several baseline (audio-only) de-reverberation methods on speech enhancement, recognition, and speaker verification problems using both synthetic and real data.
Several ablations of the proposed method are compared, along with some quantitative error analysis investigating the effects of distance and environment.
The proposed method appears to consistently improve on speech recognition and speaker verification, and perform about on par with prior work on speech enhancement (as measured by PESQ).
reviews.main_review:
This paper takes an interesting and novel approach to de-reverberation.
The paper is well written, and each component of the method is well justified.
The qualitative analysis (e.g. figure 4) is a nice touch, and helps shed some light on the behavior of the proposed method (and baselines).
That said, I do have a few questions and suggestions.

My primary criticism is in the reporting of the evaluations (tables 1, 2, 3).
Each setting is summarized by a single average score, and the "best" number is written in bold with no discussion given to the distribution of scores on the test data or the practical meaning of score differences.
This may give readers an inflated sense of performance here: is PESQ=2.38 really any different from PESQ=2.37? 2.33?  (Likewise for WER, EER, etc.)
There is a brief mention of astronomically low p-values (1.56e-60?) which I expect are primarily driven by the size of the test set, and not the size of the effect.
I would strongly encourage the authors to do a more responsible job of reporting their results: include some error bars, or some other indication of the spread of the data!

The proposed method takes an interesting approach to optimizing both magnitude and phase (eqs 1 and 2) for the output spectrogram.
The Griffin-Lim algorithm is then used to refine the phase estimate, with a vague statement of this working better than directly using predicted phase or GL with random initialization.
While I won't ask for more experiments on this, I think the point deserves a bit more explanation than what is given here, especially since there is no ablation study on the phase reconstruction loss.  (Nor is there any discussion of hyper-parameter tuning for equation 4 beyond the settings reported in the appendix.)


I found Figure 5 (qualitative examples) to be pretty difficult to parse, and I say this as a reader with (probably) above-average experience reading spectrograms.
With a bit of squinting I can see what the authors are getting at, but it would be easier to read if the spectrograms were A) larger, and B) plotted on a logarithmic frequency axis so that the low frequencies (where most of the action is) occupy more of the visual real estate.


Finally, it was nice to see the results in table 3, which show quite a strong effect on the performance of the system depending on differences in distance and room geometry.
Not much is said about the geometric differences in the environments though, either for real or simulated data.
In particular, it looks from figure 2 as though there may be configurations of speaker/listener that have no direct path, in which one might say that everything observed is "reverb".
If this is the case, it would be interesting to see how performance (table 1) differs (if at all) in the presence or absence of a direct path.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I enjoyed this paper!  I think some of the reporting could be done better, but this is a minor complaint.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper describes an audio de-reverberation method which integrates (panoramic, rgb+depth map) visual input with a spectrogram U-Net to estimate a de-reverberated spectrogram and recover the original clean signal.
The proposed method is compared to several baseline (audio-only) de-reverberation methods on speech enhancement, recognition, and speaker verification problems using both synthetic and real data.
Several ablations of the proposed method are compared, along with some quantitative error analysis investigating the effects of distance and environment.
The proposed method appears to consistently improve on speech recognition and speaker verification, and perform about on par with prior work on speech enhancement (as measured by PESQ).

Main Review:
This paper takes an interesting and novel approach to de-reverberation.
The paper is well written, and each component of the method is well justified.
The qualitative analysis (e.g. figure 4) is a nice touch, and helps shed some light on the behavior of the proposed method (and baselines).
That said, I do have a few questions and suggestions.

My primary criticism is in the reporting of the evaluations (tables 1, 2, 3).
Each setting is summarized by a single average score, and the "best" number is written in bold with no discussion given to the distribution of scores on the test data or the practical meaning of score differences.
This may give readers an inflated sense of performance here: is PESQ=2.38 really any different from PESQ=2.37? 2.33?  (Likewise for WER, EER, etc.)
There is a brief mention of astronomically low p-values (1.56e-60?) which I expect are primarily driven by the size of the test set, and not the size of the effect.
I would strongly encourage the authors to do a more responsible job of reporting their results: include some error bars, or some other indication of the spread of the data!

The proposed method takes an interesting approach to optimizing both magnitude and phase (eqs 1 and 2) for the output spectrogram.
The Griffin-Lim algorithm is then used to refine the phase estimate, with a vague statement of this working better than directly using predicted phase or GL with random initialization.
While I won't ask for more experiments on this, I think the point deserves a bit more explanation than what is given here, especially since there is no ablation study on the phase reconstruction loss.  (Nor is there any discussion of hyper-parameter tuning for equation 4 beyond the settings reported in the appendix.)


I found Figure 5 (qualitative examples) to be pretty difficult to parse, and I say this as a reader with (probably) above-average experience reading spectrograms.
With a bit of squinting I can see what the authors are getting at, but it would be easier to read if the spectrograms were A) larger, and B) plotted on a logarithmic frequency axis so that the low frequencies (where most of the action is) occupy more of the visual real estate.


Finally, it was nice to see the results in table 3, which show quite a strong effect on the performance of the system depending on differences in distance and room geometry.
Not much is said about the geometric differences in the environments though, either for real or simulated data.
In particular, it looks from figure 2 as though there may be configurations of speaker/listener that have no direct path, in which one might say that everything observed is "reverb".
If this is the case, it would be interesting to see how performance (table 1) differs (if at all) in the presence or absence of a direct path.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I enjoyed this paper!  I think some of the reporting could be done better, but this is a minor complaint.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
The authors present a de-reverberation framework that fuses panoramic RGB-D visual cues with an audio spectrogram U-Net to predict a de-reverberated spectrogram and reconstruct the clean waveform. They benchmark this multimodal approach against several audio-only de-reverberation techniques on three tasks—speech enhancement, speech recognition, and speaker verification—using both simulated and real-world recordings. In addition to full-system comparisons, the paper includes ablation studies of the proposed components and a quantitative analysis examining how distance and room characteristics influence performance. The results show consistent gains in recognition accuracy and speaker verification, while maintaining competitive speech enhancement scores (PESQ) versus prior work.

Main Review:  
This paper tackles the de-reverberation problem in a novel way by incorporating visual context, and it is generally well articulated—each module is clearly motivated, and the qualitative visualizations (e.g., Figure 4) help illustrate the method’s behavior. That said, I have several questions and suggestions:

1. Reporting of Evaluation Results  
   - In Tables 1–3, each experimental condition is summarized by a single mean score, and the best figures are simply bolded. There is no indication of variability across test samples or discussion of whether small numerical differences are meaningful (for example, is PESQ = 2.38 truly distinguishable from 2.37? Similarly for WER or EER).  
   - The brief mention of extremely low p-values (e.g., 1.56e-60) likely reflects the large size of the test set rather than a substantial effect. I strongly recommend including error bars or confidence intervals, or reporting standard deviations, so readers can judge the practical significance of these improvements.

2. Phase Reconstruction Details  
   - The paper’s dual loss on magnitude and phase (Equations 1 and 2) is interesting, and using Griffin-Lim for further phase refinement is sensible. However, the claim that this refinement “works better” than directly using the predicted phase or random-init Griffin-Lim is given without supporting experiments.  
   - Given that there is no dedicated ablation on the phase loss component, nor detailed discussion of how the weights in Equation 4 were chosen (beyond a brief appendix mention), I believe a deeper explanation or some empirical evidence would strengthen the paper.

3. Clarity of Spectrogram Visuals (Figure 5)  
   - Even for someone experienced with spectrograms, the examples in Figure 5 are hard to parse. Enlarging these plots and switching to a logarithmic frequency axis—so that low frequencies occupy more of the display—would make it much easier to see the differences between reverberant, baseline, and de-reverberated signals.

4. Role of Room Geometry and Direct Path (Table 3)  
   - It is encouraging to see in Table 3 how distance and room layout affect performance, but the manuscript provides little detail on the specific geometric variations in either the simulated or real settings.  
   - In Figure 2, some speaker–listener configurations appear to lack any direct line of sight, meaning all observed energy is reflected. It would be informative to know if performance in Table 1 degrades more severely in setups without a direct path, compared to those where one exists.

Overall, this is a well‐structured and promising paper. My suggestions mainly concern more thorough reporting and a bit more detail around phase reconstruction and environmental configurations—issues that can be addressed without altering the core contributions.

Rating: 8: accept, good paper

The Griffin-Lim algorithm is then used to refine the phase estimate, with a vague statement of this working better than directly using predicted phase or GL with random initialization.
While I won't ask for more experiments on this, I think the point deserves a bit more explanation than what is given here, especially since there is no ablation study on the phase reconstruction loss.  (Nor is there any discussion of hyper-parameter tuning for equation 4 beyond the settings reported in the appendix.)


I found Figure 5 (qualitative examples) to be pretty difficult to parse, and I say this as a reader with (probably) above-average experience reading spectrograms.
With a bit of squinting I can see what the authors are getting at, but it would be easier to read if the spectrograms were A) larger, and B) plotted on a logarithmic frequency axis so that the low frequencies (where most of the action is) occupy more of the visual real estate.


Finally, it was nice to see the results in table 3, which show quite a strong effect on the performance of the system depending on differences in distance and room geometry.
Not much is said about the geometric differences in the environments though, either for real or simulated data.
In particular, it looks from figure 2 as though there may be configurations of speaker/listener that have no direct path, in which one might say that everything observed is "reverb".
If this is the case, it would be interesting to see how performance (table 1) differs (if at all) in the presence or absence of a direct path.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I enjoyed this paper!  I think some of the reporting could be done better, but this is a minor complaint.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
In this paper, the authors introduce a novel audio-visual dereverberation approach. They propose a Visually-Informed Dereverberation of Audio (VIDA) model for dereverberation. The authors also create synthetic/simulated datasets and real-world data for experimentation. Finally, they show the impact of the proposed VIDA model on several speech tasks including, recognition, enhancement, and speaker verification. The results are encouraging. The main contribution of this work is the use of visual information as an auxiliary input for dereverberation.

Main Review:
While authors suggest visual information is helpful, it isn't reflected in the results. In Table 1, if we compare the "Audio-only dereverb." and "VIDA" results, the gains are very modest. The PESQ 2.32 to 2.37, WER-FT 3.76 to 3.66, and SID EER 2.61 to 2.40. This raises the question about the effectiveness of the proposed approach. I feel the majority of the dereverberation is still learned by the audio model. This should be investigated. 

Overall, the technical contribution is limited to a new dataset and the addition of ResNet for visual information processing. Similar approaches have been used in the audio-visual enhancement with lip information.

Summary Of The Review:
I believe the manuscript in its current form offers a very limited contribution to the research community, and the contribution of visual information in dereverberation is minimal.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript presents an audio-visual dereverberation framework called VIDA (Visually-Informed Dereverberation of Audio). The authors build both simulated and real-world reverberant speech datasets, integrate a ResNet-based visual encoder alongside an audio dereverberation network, and evaluate the model on downstream tasks including speech recognition, enhancement, and speaker verification. The central claim is that supplementing audio with visual input leads to better dereverberation.

Main Review  
The paper’s premise—that visual cues materially aid dereverberation—is not strongly supported by the reported results. In Table 1, the performance gap between the audio-only baseline and VIDA is quite small: PESQ improves from 2.32 to 2.37, WER-FT from 3.76% to 3.66%, and speaker ID EER from 2.61% to 2.40%. Such marginal gains call into question how much the video stream actually contributes versus the audio model itself. A more thorough analysis isolating the visual encoder’s effect would help clarify whether the model is genuinely leveraging visual information or simply relying on the audio component.

Beyond the empirical results, the technical innovation appears modest. The authors’ main additions are (1) a new reverberant speech corpus and (2) incorporation of a standard ResNet for visual feature extraction. Prior work in audio-visual enhancement has already employed similar lip-based visual processing, making the novelty here incremental.

Summary of the Review  
While the idea of using visual data for dereverberation is interesting, the current results show only minimal improvements, and the architectural contributions are limited. In its present form, this work offers a rather small advance to the research community.

Rating  
3: reject, not good enough
