title: Layer-wise Adaptive Model Aggregation for Scalable Federated Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a novel adaptive model aggregating schema under a federated learning setting, where it effective reduces the communication overhead without significantly sacrificing the generalization performance. Both empirical study and theoretical analysis are included to justify the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method.
reviews.main_review:
Effectively reducing the communication overhead is an important problem for federated learning, with the recent advance of layer-wise model freezing training, this paper manages to extend this idea for network traffic reduction in federated learning. Concretely, I list the main strengths and minor weakness of this paper as below:

Advantages:

+ To leverage layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is a simple but novel idea. According to the empirical study, this method seems to be effective and efficient. 
+ The paper also includes tight convergence analysis for the proposed method. 

Disadvantages:
+ The communication cost calculated in Section 6 is a little sloppy, in practices, there are plenty of system advances managing to hide the communication overhead within the computation slots, e.g. PyTorch-DDP (https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.15704). In this fashion, the actual runtime may not be simply linear to the amount of network traffic. Perhaps, a more sophisticated cost model would consider both the computation power of the edge device and network condition during training, then model the cost including the mechanism of overlapping communication and computation to estimate the actual runtime.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The idea of utilizing layer-wise model freezing for federated learning is an interesting idea, the paper includes reasonable empirical study and convergence analysis to illustrate the effectiveness of this proposed approach. 
Although some part of the cost model in the simulation is a little sloppy, I tend to advocate the acceptance of this paper.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This work presents an adaptive aggregation scheme for federated learning that selectively freezes layers to cut down on communication overhead, while still preserving model generalization. The authors validate their approach through both empirical experiments and a rigorous convergence analysis.

Detailed Review  
Reducing communication costs in federated learning is a critical challenge, and building on recent advances in layer-wise freezing, this paper adapts that idea to limit network traffic. Below I outline its primary strengths and a notable weakness.

Strengths:  
1. Introducing layer-wise model freezing into the federated learning framework is an elegant yet original contribution. The experimental results suggest that it delivers substantial communication savings without degrading accuracy.  
2. The theoretical section offers a tight convergence proof for the proposed algorithm, lending strong analytical support to the empirical findings.

Weakness:  
• The communication-cost model in Section 6 is somewhat simplistic. In real-world deployments, frameworks like PyTorch-DDP (https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.15704) can overlap communication with computation, so wall-clock runtime does not scale linearly with transmitted bytes. To better reflect practical performance, the authors should consider a more nuanced cost model that incorporates edge-device compute capabilities, varying network conditions, and the degree of overlap between communication and computation.

Overall Recommendation  
Leveraging layer-wise freezing to curb communication in federated learning is a compelling idea, and the paper backs it up with sound experiments and tight convergence guarantees. Despite the somewhat coarse cost model, I believe this work makes a valuable contribution and recommend acceptance.

Rating  
8: accept, good paper

In this fashion, the actual runtime may not be simply linear to the amount of network traffic. reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes an adaptive interval schema for layer-wise model aggregation in federated settings. The aim of the proposed method is to reduce communication costs by adaptively decreasing the frequency of layer-wise aggregation with consideration of model discrepancy.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

1. It is an interesting idea to adjust the aggregation interval for layer-wise aggregation.
2. Detailed theoretical analysis and experiments are conducted.
3. The paper is well-written.


Weaknesses:

1. It is uncertain that the proposed method can really reduce communication costs. Due to the reduced frequency of aggregation and adopt layer-wise partial model aggregation strategy in each round, it is very likely that the proposed method will decrease the convergence speed of the global model. Therefore, the proposed method needs more communication rounds to achieve a convergence state, thus the total communication costs are still very high. Moreover, at each communication round, the client still needs to download the full model from the server. Therefore, the downloading communication costs haven't been improved. 
2. In the convergence analysis, Assumption 4 is a little tricky, and helps to avoid much detailed and further analysis. The convergence rate is O(1/m^½ K^½), which is loose compared to some convergence analysis of FedAvg, such as ON THE CONVERGENCE OF FEDAVG ON NON-IID DATA (https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.02189).
3. The experiment is insufficient to support their claims. For example, the only baseline is FedAvg, and it is necessary to choose some compression methods as a baseline. Moreover, the communication cost should consider the number of communication rounds to achieve a convergence state.


Questions and suggestions:

1. Algorithm 1 should explicitly state which steps are executed on the server or client. For example, line 3 should be on the client, and lines from 4 to 10 are on the server. Moreover, it should explicitly highlight when the communication occurs.
2. It is necessary to draw the convergence curves of the proposed methods and baselines.
3. It is unclear how to construct non-IID scenarios in the experiment setting.
4. There are some federated learning methods that only update parts of its model. This paper should also consider these baseline methods. 

http://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/collins21a/collins21a.pdf

https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.07623

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes an adaptive interval schema for layer-wise model aggregation in federated settings. The aim of the proposed method is to reduce communication costs by adaptively decreasing the frequency of layer-wise aggregation with consideration of model discrepancy.

Main Review:
Strengths:

1. It is an interesting idea to adjust the aggregation interval for layer-wise aggregation.
2. Detailed theoretical analysis and experiments are conducted.
3. The paper is well-written.


Weaknesses:

1. It is uncertain that the proposed method can really reduce communication costs. Due to the reduced frequency of aggregation and adopt layer-wise partial model aggregation strategy in each round, it is very likely that the proposed method will decrease the convergence speed of the global model. Therefore, the proposed method needs more communication rounds to achieve a convergence state, thus the total communication costs are still very high. Moreover, at each communication round, the client still needs to download the full model from the server. Therefore, the downloading communication costs haven't been improved. 
2. In the convergence analysis, Assumption 4 is a little tricky, and helps to avoid much detailed and further analysis. The convergence rate is O(1/m^½ K^½), which is loose compared to some convergence analysis of FedAvg, such as ON THE CONVERGENCE OF FEDAVG ON NON-IID DATA (https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.02189).
3. The experiment is insufficient to support their claims. For example, the only baseline is FedAvg, and it is necessary to choose some compression methods as a baseline. Moreover, the communication cost should consider the number of communication rounds to achieve a convergence state.


Questions and suggestions:

1. Algorithm 1 should explicitly state which steps are executed on the server or client. For example, line 3 should be on the client, and lines from 4 to 10 are on the server. Moreover, it should explicitly highlight when the communication occurs.
2. It is necessary to draw the convergence curves of the proposed methods and baselines.
3. It is unclear how to construct non-IID scenarios in the experiment setting.
4. There are some federated learning methods that only update parts of its model. This paper should also consider these baseline methods. 

http://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/collins21a/collins21a.pdf

https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.07623

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposes an interesting layer-wise model aggregation method to reduce communication cost while trying to keep the same performance with FedAvg. However, the proposed method’s design, theoretical analysis and experiments cannot support its claim.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript presents an adaptive-interval approach for layer-wise aggregation in federated learning. By monitoring inter-layer discrepancies, the method dynamically prolongs aggregation intervals to purportedly cut down on communication overhead.

Strengths  
1. The notion of tuning aggregation intervals on a per-layer basis is an intriguing and novel direction.  
2. The authors back their proposal with a fairly detailed theoretical derivation and supporting experiments.  
3. The writing is clear and the presentation is generally well organized.

Weaknesses  
1. It is not obvious that this scheme truly reduces end-to-end communication. Because aggregation happens less often and only a subset of layers is exchanged each round, convergence may slow down. Slower convergence typically necessitates more rounds, which could negate any per-round savings. In addition, every client still downloads the full global model in each round, so the download cost remains unchanged.  
2. The convergence analysis hinges on Assumption 4, which appears somewhat contrived and seems to shortcut a more rigorous proof. The established rate of O(1/√(mK)) is relatively loose when compared with tighter convergence guarantees for FedAvg under non-IID data (e.g., “On the Convergence of FedAvg on Non-IID Data,” arXiv:1907.02189).  
3. The experimental evaluation is too limited to validate the paper’s main claims. Only vanilla FedAvg is used as a baseline; compression-based or partial-update FL methods should be included for a fairer comparison. Furthermore, communication efficiency should be measured by the total number of rounds required to hit a target accuracy, not just per-round cost.

Questions and Suggestions  
1. In Algorithm 1, please make it explicit which steps run on the client side versus the server side (for instance, line 3 on clients, lines 4–10 on the server) and clearly mark where communication takes place.  
2. Provide training curves that plot accuracy (or loss) versus communication rounds for both the proposed method and all baselines.  
3. Describe in detail how the non-IID data partitions are constructed in your experiments.  
4. Include federated learning methods that also update only parts of the model as additional baselines. Relevant examples include:  
   – Collins et al. (2021), http://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/collins21a/collins21a.pdf  
   – [Another partial-update method], https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.07623

Overall Assessment  
The idea of layer-wise adaptive aggregation is promising, but as presented, the method’s design, theoretical justification, and experimental support do not convincingly demonstrate a real communication advantage over existing approaches.

Rating  
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: In this paper, the authors propose a layer-wise model aggregation scheme in federated learning cases to reduce the communication cost. Specifically, they quantified the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusted the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner. By increasing the aggregation intervals and relaxing the aggregation frequency, the method can reduce the communication cost in federated learning cases. The experimental results show it can reduce the communication cost for IID data and non-IID data compared to FedAvg.
reviews.main_review:
They provide a communication-efficient federated optimization and relax the aggregation frequency by quantifying the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusting the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner.

Some questions are the following.

layerwise asynchronous model update and temporally weighted aggregation." IEEE transactions on neural

It is not clear about absolute earnings with sacrificing the accuracy. In some cases, especially in industry, the model's accuracy matters more than training time (e.g., the total training time is within several hours.)

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
The authors introduce FedLAMA, a layer-wise adaptive aggregation strategy for federated learning aimed at cutting down communication overhead. They measure the divergence between each local model layer and the corresponding global model layer, then adjust aggregation intervals per layer based on this discrepancy. By allowing less frequent aggregation where models are more similar, the approach claims to lower communication costs. Experimental results indicate that FedLAMA can reduce communication for both IID and non-IID data relative to the standard FedAvg.

Detailed Comments  
The core idea—quantifying layer-level model divergence and tuning aggregation intervals accordingly—is appealing and theoretically grounded. However, I have two main concerns:

1) Limited Baselines  
   - The authors only benchmark FedLAMA against FedAvg. To demonstrate broader competitiveness, comparison with other periodic or adaptive aggregation methods is needed. Examples include FedProx, FedNova, SCAFFOLD, and both synchronous and asynchronous protocols. In particular, relevant works such as:  
     [1] Chen, Y., Sun, X., & Jin, Y. (2019). “Communication-efficient federated deep learning with layerwise asynchronous model update and temporally weighted aggregation,” IEEE T-NNLS.  
     [2] Xie, C., Koyejo, S., & Gupta, I. (2019). “Asynchronous federated optimization,” arXiv:1903.03934.  
   - Without these comparisons, it’s hard to judge whether the gains of FedLAMA are substantial beyond the FedAvg baseline.

2) Trade-off Between Communication Savings and Accuracy  
   - The experiments report only relative reductions in communication but do not present absolute measures of communication time or rounds.  
   - Moreover, FedLAMA appears to incur a slight accuracy drop in the results shown. In many real-world or industrial scenarios, maintaining model performance is more critical than saving a few communication rounds, especially when total training time remains modest (e.g., within hours).  
   - The paper should clarify the end-to-end communication time savings and offer a more detailed analysis of accuracy versus communication trade-offs.

Overall Assessment  
The idea of layer-wise adaptive aggregation is interesting and supported by some theoretical discussion. Still, the empirical evaluation is too narrow to prove its practical value. A richer set of baselines and a clearer account of absolute communication costs versus accuracy impacts are necessary before this work can be fully endorsed.

Rating  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
In this paper, the authors propose a layer-wise model aggregation scheme in federated learning cases to reduce the communication cost. Specifically, they quantified the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusted the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner. By increasing the aggregation intervals and relaxing the aggregation frequency, the method can reduce the communication cost in federated learning cases. The experimental results show it can reduce the communication cost for IID data and non-IID data compared to FedAvg.

Main Review:
They provide a communication-efficient federated optimization and relax the aggregation frequency by quantifying the model discrepancy between local models and global models and adaptively adjusting the aggregation interval in a layer-wise manner.

Some questions are the following.

1）The baseline is FedAvg, too few. Why not compare with other the periodic full aggregation scheme (e.g., FedProx, FedNova, SCAFFOLD, etc.), even some synchronized schemes? the synchronized schemes refer to,

[1] Chen, Yang, Xiaoyan Sun, and Yaochu Jin. "Communication-efficient federated deep learning with
layerwise asynchronous model update and temporally weighted aggregation." IEEE transactions on neural
networks and learning systems 31.10 (2019): 4229-4238.

[2] Xie, C., Koyejo, S., & Gupta, I. (2019). Asynchronous federated optimization. arXiv preprint
arXiv:1903.03934. 

2）FedLAMA seems to lead to some accuracy drop as the experiments show. They do not show the total time in terms of communication cost, just show the reduced ratio for communication. It is not clear about absolute earnings with sacrificing the accuracy. In some cases, especially in industry, the model's accuracy matters more than training time (e.g., the total training time is within several hours.)

Summary Of The Review:
Thanks to the authors for their efforts for their theoretical analysis and experimentally proof of the proposed federated optimization scheme. The experimental results are a little weak to show the effectiveness and the practicality.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
Summary of the Submission:
The authors introduce an adaptive aggregation strategy tailored for federated learning. Their theoretical investigation explores how the choice of aggregation interval impacts the convergence rate. Empirical results demonstrate that the proposed scheme can lower communication overhead.

Detailed Review:

Strengths:
1. The topic is significant and the concept they propose is original.
2. They derive a concrete convergence rate for their method.
3. A comprehensive set of experiments supports their claims.

Weaknesses:
1. The theoretical development contains mistakes. In particular, Equation (15) does not hold, and Equation (25) is likewise incorrect.
2. In Lemma 5.2, an explicit condition on the stepsize η is missing and should be stated.
3. The upper bound on convergence is actually looser than that of the standard FedAvg algorithm. While Remark 1 asserts linear speedup when K=O(m³), the result in [1] achieves this with K=O(m), indicating that the bound here is substantially weaker.

Reference:
[1] On the Linear Speedup Analysis of Communication Efficient Momentum SGD for Distributed Non-Convex Optimization

Overall Assessment:
The paper offers a promising direction for communication reduction in federated learning, but its theoretical analysis has critical gaps that must be addressed before it can be accepted.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the generalization of iterative noisy learning algorithms. First of all, the authors provide a new generalization bound based on the Hellinger distance. This bound is basically based on the Hellinger distance between the output of the algorithm when one of the points in the training set is replaced with a fresh sample. They they use this bound to provide a generalization error guarantee for a broad class of noisy iterative algorithm which is defined as W_t = W_{t-1} - eta_t, where eta_t conditioned on the past iterated follows an exponential family distribution which for instance include SGLD. For this broad class, they provide a unified generalization bound that includes SGLD and Noisy Sign-SGD. Finally, the authors provide numerical study of their bound for several benchmarks and show that their bound achieves better estimate of generalization error.
reviews.main_review:
- Using the generalization bound in Theorem: In proposition 1, the authors provide a generalization bound based on the distributional stability in terms of Hellinger distance. However, in most of the results in the paper they mainly use the upper bound based on KL divergence (Prop. 2). Is that correct?  

- The dependence of the learning rate on the optimization trajectory: In your bound alpha_t depends on the dataset and trajectory. It basically depends on some sort of Lipschitz constant of the loss function. For instance in SGLD, alpha_t depends on the learning rate and inverse temperatures. Therefore, I think it can limit us to use very small learning rates. Also, how did you make sure that this constraint is satisfied in your numerical studies?

- The comparison with Rodríguez-Gálvez et al.: Your bound is very similar to the generalization bound in Haghifam et al'2020. As you mentioned the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is stated for LD algorithm. Recently, the bound in Haghifam et al'2020 is extended to SGLD in [1]. The bound in Rodríguez-Gálvez et al has worse dependence on n (1/sqrt(n)), however, they have a decaying factor in their bound to compensate using of the chain rule. It would be nice to numerically compare your bound with the bound in [1]. 

- Applying your framework for analyzing SGD:  What are the roadblocks for applying your bound to analyzing SGD?

- Surrogate loss: I think you should distinguish the loss function that we use to measure the generalization error with the loss function that is used for training. You should use different notations. 


[1] "On random subset generalization error bounds and the stochastic gradient langevin dynamics algorithm", Borja Rodríguez-Gálvez, Germán Bassi, Ragnar Thobaben, Mikael Skoglund

Summary Of The Review:
Very interesting and solid contribution!

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary of the Submission:
This manuscript investigates the generalization behavior of a wide class of noisy, iterative learning procedures. The authors first derive a novel generalization bound expressed via the Hellinger distance between the algorithm’s outputs when a single training example is replaced. Building on this, they specialize the result to any iterative update of the form Wₜ = Wₜ₋₁ – ηₜ, where the noise term ηₜ (conditioned on past iterates) follows an exponential-family distribution—capturing algorithms such as SGLD and Noisy Sign-SGD. They present a unified generalization error bound that simultaneously covers these instances, and they complement their theoretical findings with empirical evaluations, demonstrating that their bound yields tighter estimates of generalization error on several benchmarks.

Detailed Comments:
1. Role of the Hellinger‐based Bound versus the KL‐based Upper Bound  
   • In Proposition 1 you introduce your primary generalization guarantee in terms of Hellinger‐distance stability. However, most of the subsequent corollaries and examples seem to rely on the looser KL‐divergence–based bound from Proposition 2. Could you clarify if this is indeed the case, and if so, explain the trade‐off that leads you to favor the KL version in applications?

2. Dependence of αₜ on the Optimization Path  
   • Your bound involves factors αₜ that depend on the data trajectory—effectively requiring a Lipschitz constant of the loss along the optimization path. For methods like SGLD, αₜ is tied to the step size and the inverse temperature. This condition could force very small learning rates to satisfy the stability requirement. How did you verify that these αₜ constraints held true in your numerical experiments? Could you comment on how restrictive this is in practice?

3. Comparison to Rodríguez‐Gálvez et al.  
   • The form of your bound closely parallels the results in Haghifam et al. (2020) for Langevin Dynamics, which was recently extended to SGLD in Rodríguez‐Gálvez et al. [1]. While their bound degrades as O(1/√n), they introduce a decaying factor via repeated use of the chain rule. It would strengthen the paper to include a direct numerical comparison between your bound and that in [1], highlighting in which regimes each bound is tighter.

4. Extending to Plain SGD  
   • You focus on exponential‐family noise processes. Could you discuss the main obstacles to applying your Hellinger‐distance framework to standard (deterministic) SGD? What prevents a direct extension, and what modifications might be needed?

5. Notational Distinction for Surrogate Loss  
   • Throughout, it would help readability to use distinct symbols for (i) the loss employed during training (which may include surrogate or regularization terms) and (ii) the loss function used solely for evaluating generalization error. This clearer notation would prevent any confusion between these two roles.

Overall Assessment:
This work offers a thoughtful and technically solid contribution to our understanding of how noisy iterative algorithms generalize. The Hellinger‐distance perspective is novel in this context, and the unified treatment of SGLD and related methods is valuable. The empirical results corroborate the theoretical claims, showing improved estimates of the true generalization error.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

[1] “On random subset generalization error bounds and the stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics algorithm,” Borja Rodríguez‐Gálvez, Germán Bassi, Ragnar Thobaben, Mikael Skoglund.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper improves generalization bounds based on the notion of stability. The notion of stability measures the effect of resampling a data point to the output of the algorithm. The paper improves known analyses in two directions: 
- It proposes to use the notion of expected stability rather than uniform stability, which should to be tighter.
- It generalizes the analysis to noises in an exponential family rather than Gaussian noise.
reviews.main_review:
This paper provides several technical improvements to a recent line of work that I am not familiar with. Thus I can hardly review that relevance  and novelty of these improvements (and I am thus putting a low confidence score). I am concerned by the fact that the article is hard to read in a self-contained way. The authors largely comment the articulation of their work with similar works, but almost never comment the relevance of their work for a wider community. 

Another concern is that the article provides two improvements listed above, but I could not perceive why it was coherent to present them in a common article. This might add some confusion to the paper. 

As a naive question, I would like to ask why expected stability bounds are preferred. It seems that they involve expectations that are hard to compute analytically, at best one can approximate these expectations by sampling. How should these bounds be used in the end? In practice, to choose stopping criteria for algorithms, for instance? Or in theory, to analyze the performance of some algorithms?

I found the generalization from Gaussian noise to exponential families novel and interesting.

In Section 2, I find difficult to determine what are your contributions and what was already known. For instance, Proposition 2 seems well-known right? Why not giving some references rather than proving it? Similarly, to what extent was Proposition 1 known?

Minor comments:
-  Section 2: "D be an unknown distribution OVER Z" (not "of")
- Why do you introduce the notation W_{0:(t-1)} = w_{0:(t-1)}? I am under the impression that this is twice the same object, but I might misunderstand.
- You sometimes use the notation KL(P,Q) and KL(P||Q).
- Conflict of notation between S_0 and S_n (for n=0!).

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Submission:  
The manuscript sharpens generalization bounds based on algorithmic stability. Specifically, it advances prior work in two ways:  
1. It replaces uniform stability with an expected‐stability framework, which should yield tighter bounds.  
2. It extends the analysis of added noise from the Gaussian case to arbitrary exponential‐family distributions.  

Detailed Review:  
While the paper offers several technical refinements to a series of recent results, I am not sufficiently familiar with this literature to fully assess the significance and novelty of these advances (hence my low confidence rating). My primary concerns are:

1. Readability and Scope  
   - The paper assumes considerable background and does not clearly explain its relevance to a broader audience. It frequently situates itself relative to related work but rarely spells out why these improvements matter outside the immediate circle of stability analyses.  
   - It is not obvious why the two contributions—expected stability and exponential‐family noise—belong in a single manuscript. Presenting them together feels somewhat arbitrary and may confuse readers about the overarching theme.  

2. Practical Use of Expected Stability  
   - It would help to understand why one would prefer expected stability bounds in practice. These bounds involve expectations that seem intractable to compute exactly; at best, one could approximate via Monte Carlo. What is the intended use? Are they meant to guide stopping criteria in iterative algorithms, or to furnish tighter theoretical guarantees for specific methods?  

3. Novelty of the Exponential‐Family Extension  
   - On the other hand, I find the leap from Gaussian noise to general exponential families both original and interesting. This is a clear technical contribution that could broaden the applicability of stability‐based bounds.  

4. Clarity of Contributions in Section 2  
   - It is hard to distinguish which results are new and which are restatements of known facts. For example, Proposition 2 appears to be well‐established; a citation might suffice rather than a full proof. Similarly, the novelty of Proposition 1 is not made explicit—have parts of it appeared elsewhere?  

Minor Points:  
- In Section 2, write “D be an unknown distribution over Z” (rather than “of Z”).  
- The notation W_{0:(t−1)} = w_{0:(t−1)} seems redundant—are these two distinct objects or the same?  
- The paper sometimes writes KL(P,Q) and elsewhere KL(P∥Q); choose one convention.  
- There is a clash between notation S₀ and Sₙ when n = 0.  

Overall Assessment:  
I am not confident in my evaluation, given the technical nature of the work and my limited familiarity with this line of research. I would welcome further clarification on the practical applications of expected stability and a more compelling rationale for combining the two main contributions in one paper.  

Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper provides generalization guarantees for exponential family sgld which is a more general setup than standard gaussian noise langevin dynamics as it allows for noise settings from any member of the exponential family. The authors point out that the setup they consider is a strict generalization of the standard langevin dynamics, and give another explicit example of sign sgd based generalization. 

The authors use and show gradient discrepancy as opposed to gradient norms as used by Li et al, and gradient incoherence as used by Negrea et al. but the latter have 1/\sqrt{n}. Overall, from useful empirical observations it seems that the bounds proposed by the authors are tighter than these other bounds.
reviews.main_review:
There have been several works that generalize from gaussian settings to the more general exponential family, including the ones that are cited by the authors but also for example monotone retargetting and exponential family PCA. As such there are several tools for such an analysis that are pretty well known now. Other than that, the analysis follows closely with that of Li et al, with few minor but important differences. 

I am not sure if the extension to exponential family is a major contribution. It allows for analysis of signSGD. But given several other works of similar nature, I am not sure if the analysis and the major contribution as claimed, including in the title of the paper justifies a publication. 

It seems, however, that proposing gradient discrepancy itself is a useful contribution. Can the authors highlight the difference in the analysis from Li et al explicitly that led to this difference in the bounds ? Why is this sidelined as a side-effect? Does this follow from the new exponential family based analysis proposed here (it does not seem to be so) ? The experiments are also conducted based on this contribution. As such the main message/contribution of the paper and the empirical section seems disconnected. I am happy to be corrected.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The main contribution of exponential family dynamics seems not very significant. Gradient discrepancy is sidelined as a minor contribution but is focussed on a lot more in the empirical section, which makes the paper seems disconnected.

Can the authors highlight the difference in the analysis from Li et al explicitly that led to this difference in the bounds ? Why is this sidelined as a side-effect? Does this follow from the new exponential family based analysis proposed here (it does not seem to be so) ? The experiments are also conducted based on this contribution. As such the main message/contribution of the paper and the empirical section seems disconnected. I am happy to be corrected.

Summary Of The Review:
The main contribution of exponential family dynamics seems not very significant. Gradient discrepancy is sidelined as a minor contribution but is focussed on a lot more in the empirical section, which makes the paper seems disconnected.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This manuscript studies generalization guarantees for stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (SGLD) driven by noise sampled from a general exponential family, extending the classic Gaussian-noise Langevin framework. The authors emphasize that their setting strictly encompasses standard Langevin dynamics and provide an additional illustrative example based on signSGD. Rather than relying on gradient norms (as in Li et al.) or gradient incoherence (as in Negrea et al., which scales as 1/√n), they introduce and leverage a notion of gradient discrepancy. Empirically, the proposed bounds appear to be tighter than those in prior work.

Detailed Comments  
1. Novelty of Exponential-Family Extension  
   - While the paper correctly notes that moving beyond Gaussian noise to exponential-family noise is more general, there already exists a body of literature (including some cited by the authors) on such extensions—examples include monotone reparameterization techniques and exponential-family PCA. Many analytical tools for handling exponential-family distributions are well established. Apart from a few minor tweaks, the theoretical development here parallels Li et al.’s analysis closely. It is therefore unclear whether this extension constitutes a substantial advance.  
   - The ability to cover signSGD under this umbrella is interesting, but given the abundance of related generalizations, I question whether this alone warrants publication, especially under the weight of the paper’s claimed contributions in its title and abstract.

2. Gradient Discrepancy as a Contribution  
   - Introducing the gradient-discrepancy measure seems like a potentially useful idea. However, the manuscript treats it as a by-product of the exponential-family framework rather than a central innovation. It would strengthen the paper if the authors explicitly pointed out the analytical divergence from Li et al. that gives rise to the improved bounds. Is the gradient-discrepancy notion genuinely a consequence of the new noise model, or could it have been derived in the Gaussian case as well?  
   - In the current presentation, the connection between this theoretical contribution and the empirical results is not made sufficiently clear. Since the experiments hinge on gradient discrepancy, the paper’s narrative feels somewhat disjointed—first framing the exponential-family generalization as the main story, then pivoting to empirical benefits of gradient discrepancy.

3. Empirical Evaluation  
   - The experiments convincingly suggest that gradient-discrepancy–based bounds outperform those based on gradient norms or incoherence. However, the link back to the paper’s primary theoretical thrust—the general exponential-family setup—is underemphasized. Clarifying this link would help readers appreciate how the new noise model and the discrepancy measure interact in practice.

Overall Assessment  
The move to exponential-family noise in SGLD, while broader in scope, does not strike me as sufficiently groundbreaking given existing related work. By contrast, the gradient-discrepancy concept appears more promising but is relegated to a secondary role, creating a disconnect between the paper’s stated main contribution and its empirical focus. I encourage the authors to either elevate the discrepancy analysis to the central theme or better integrate the exponential-family arguments with their experiments.

Recommendation  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

The authors use and show gradient discrepancy as opposed to gradient norms as used by Li et al, and gradient incoherence as used by Negrea et al. but the latter have 1/\sqrt{n}. Overall, from useful empirical observations it seems that the bounds proposed by the authors are tighter than these other bounds.

Main Review:
There have been several works that generalize from gaussian settings to the more general exponential family, including the ones that are cited by the authors but also for example monotone retargetting and exponential family PCA. As such there are several tools for such an analysis that are pretty well known now. Other than that, the analysis follows closely with that of Li et al, with few minor but important differences.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper under review considers the generalized SDLG algorithms with exponential family noise introduced to the mini-batch gradient descent. The analysis of the error bound is based on the control of the expected stability. Experiments are provided to support the proposed EFLD SGD algorithms.
reviews.main_review:
Strength.
1. The paper proves the generalization error of ''existing'' noisy stochastic iterative algorithms to have a $\mathcal O(1/n)$ sample dependence. 
2. The exponential family noise is considered in the generalized SGD algorithms. The generalized error bound is based on expected stability, which seems to bring new ideas for general exponential family noise.  A thorough analysis of the error bound of exponential family noise SGD algorithms is provided. 

Weakness.

1. The proposed algorithm does not show state-of-the-art performance.  The ultimate goal of the proposed algorithms is to achieve better performance on real data sets. Without strong support from the empirical study,  it is not convincible to believe why one bothers to study such more complicated cases. 
2. Adding to the above point, the paper only reviews the SGD in the literature, there have been very recent works based on Langevin dynamics, e.g. replica-exchange Langevin dynamic SGD, second-order Hamiltonian Langevin dynamics. Those algorithms achieve state-of-the-art performance. It is questionable to ask if the newly proposed algorithms in this paper could provide better performance if the model architectures are improved.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper seems to provide a new family of SGD algorithms. The potential of the model seems to be broad. The theoretical analysis is recognized and deserves their own interest. However, the current state of the empirical study does not show promising performance.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper under review considers the generalized SDLG algorithms with exponential family noise introduced to the mini-batch gradient descent. The analysis of the error bound is based on the control of the expected stability. Experiments are provided to support the proposed EFLD SGD algorithms.

Main Review:
Strength.
1. The paper proves the generalization error of ''existing'' noisy stochastic iterative algorithms to have a $\mathcal O(1/n)$ sample dependence. 
2. The exponential family noise is considered in the generalized SGD algorithms. The generalized error bound is based on expected stability, which seems to bring new ideas for general exponential family noise.  A thorough analysis of the error bound of exponential family noise SGD algorithms is provided. 

Weakness.

1. The proposed algorithm does not show state-of-the-art performance.  The ultimate goal of the proposed algorithms is to achieve better performance on real data sets. Without strong support from the empirical study,  it is not convincible to believe why one bothers to study such more complicated cases. 
2. Adding to the above point, the paper only reviews the SGD in the literature, there have been very recent works based on Langevin dynamics, e.g. replica-exchange Langevin dynamic SGD, second-order Hamiltonian Langevin dynamics. Those algorithms achieve state-of-the-art performance. It is questionable to ask if the newly proposed algorithms in this paper could provide better performance if the model architectures are improved.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper seems to provide a new family of SGD algorithms. The potential of the model seems to be broad. The theoretical analysis is recognized and deserves their own interest. However, the current state of the empirical study does not show promising performance.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript studies a family of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) methods in which noise drawn from an exponential family is injected into the mini‐batch updates. The authors derive generalization error bounds by controlling the expected stability of these noisy iterations and present experiments to validate the proposed EFLD–SGD variants.

Main Review  

Strengths  
1. The paper rigorously shows that the generalization error for a broad class of existing noisy stochastic iterative algorithms scales as O(1/n) in the number of samples.  
2. By modeling the injected noise via an exponential family, the authors extend stability‐based error bounds to this richer noise class. The theoretical derivation is thorough and offers fresh perspectives on how exponential‐family perturbations affect convergence and generalization.

Weaknesses  
1. Empirical performance of the proposed algorithms does not match state‐of‐the‐art results. Since the ultimate objective of these methods is to improve practical performance on real datasets, the current experiments offer insufficient justification for the added algorithmic complexity.  
2. The related‐work discussion is limited to classical noisy SGD, overlooking recent advances in Langevin‐based SGD variants (e.g., replica‐exchange Langevin dynamics, second‐order Hamiltonian Langevin methods) that set the current performance benchmark. It remains unclear whether the new exponential‐family–noise schemes could outperform those methods, especially when paired with modern neural architectures.

Overall Assessment  
The paper introduces an interesting and potentially broad class of noisy SGD algorithms with solid stability‐based generalization analysis. However, the lack of compelling experimental gains undermines the practical motivation for these more elaborate schemes.

Rating  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Learning Visual-Linguistic Adequacy, Fidelity, and Fluency for Novel Object Captioning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a framework that combines masked language models (BERT) and image-text embedding models (CLIP) for novel object captioning. The proposed model significantly improves the fluency, fidelity, and adequacy of the generated images. Extensive experiments are conducted and the comparison between the proposed model and existing models shows that the proposed model performs relatively well.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

S1: This paper pays attention to three important properties of captions -- fluency, fidelity and adequacy and proposes a formwork to generate fluent, faithful and adequate captions to describe images.

S2: Extensive experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models on novel object captioning.

Weaknesses:

W1: The proposed model combines some existing models, like BERT, CLIP, so one of my concerns is the novelty of the proposed framwork. Plus, the reward to train the model is not novel, which is used in Rotian's work [1] and distilling from BERT for text generation was proposed by Chen [2]. Also, the observation that repeating the objects that occur in an image can improve retrieval score is not new [3].

W2: Neigher BERT nor CLIP is trained using the dataset used in the paper, so how to mitigate the problem of domain shift when applying BERT and CLIP? I do not think you can assume that BERT and CLIP are always correct.

W3: It seems that BERT is only used during training. I am wondering whether BERT can be used in the inference stage, for example, using the captioning model to generate a caption with beam search and then employing BERT to generate more captions. In this way, we can see whether BERT can improve fluency.

W4: Lack of human study.

[1] Rotian Luo et al.. Discriminability objective for training descriptive captions. CVPR, 2018.

[2] Y-C Chen et al.. Distilling Knowledge Learned in BERT for Text Generation. ACL, 2020.

[3] Qingzhong Wang et al.. Describing Like Humans: on Diversity in Image Captioning. CVPR, 2019.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper proposed a strong baseline of novel object captioning via combining BERT and CLIP models, however, the novelty is limited.

reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: Not applicable

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a framework that combines masked language models (BERT) and image-text embedding models (CLIP) for novel object captioning. The proposed model significantly improves the fluency, fidelity, and adequacy of the generated images. Extensive experiments are conducted and the comparison between the proposed model and existing models shows that the proposed model performs relatively well.

Main Review:
Strengths:

S1: This paper pays attention to three important properties of captions -- fluency, fidelity and adequacy and proposes a formwork to generate fluent, faithful and adequate captions to describe images.

S2: Extensive experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models on novel object captioning.

Weaknesses:

W1: The proposed model combines some existing models, like BERT, CLIP, so one of my concerns is the novelty of the proposed framwork. Plus, the reward to train the model is not novel, which is used in Rotian's work [1] and distilling from BERT for text generation was proposed by Chen [2]. Also, the observation that repeating the objects that occur in an image can improve retrieval score is not new [3].

W2: Neigher BERT nor CLIP is trained using the dataset used in the paper, so how to mitigate the problem of domain shift when applying BERT and CLIP? I do not think you can assume that BERT and CLIP are always correct.

W3: It seems that BERT is only used during training. I am wondering whether BERT can be used in the inference stage, for example, using the captioning model to generate a caption with beam search and then employing BERT to generate more captions. In this way, we can see whether BERT can improve fluency.

W4: Lack of human study.

[1] Rotian Luo et al.. Discriminability objective for training descriptive captions. CVPR, 2018.

[2] Y-C Chen et al.. Distilling Knowledge Learned in BERT for Text Generation. ACL, 2020.

[3] Qingzhong Wang et al.. Describing Like Humans: on Diversity in Image Captioning. CVPR, 2019.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposed a strong baseline of novel object captioning via combining BERT and CLIP models, however, the novelty is limited.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This work presents a novel-object captioning framework that fuses a masked language model (BERT) with an image–text embedding model (CLIP). By optimizing for three key caption qualities—fluency, fidelity, and adequacy—the authors show through extensive experimentation that their approach yields more fluent, faithful, and adequate captions than existing methods.

Strengths  
• The paper explicitly targets fluency, fidelity, and adequacy—three critical dimensions of caption quality—and proposes a cohesive pipeline to generate captions that excel along all these axes.  
• A thorough set of experiments demonstrates consistent gains over state-of-the-art novel-object captioning models, highlighting the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

Weaknesses  
• The framework largely reuses off-the-shelf components (BERT, CLIP), and the training reward mirrors prior work (e.g., Rotian et al.’s discriminability objective [1]). Distilling knowledge from BERT for text generation was already explored by Chen et al. [2], and the idea that repeating detected objects boosts retrieval performance has been reported in Wang et al. [3]. This raises concerns about the paper’s originality.  
• Since neither BERT nor CLIP are adapted to the target dataset, the issue of domain shift remains unaddressed. It is risky to assume these pre-trained models will always provide correct signals when transferred to novel-object captioning without any domain-specific tuning.  
• BERT appears to be employed exclusively during training. It would be valuable to investigate whether BERT could also enhance inference—for example, by reranking beam-search outputs to further improve fluency.  
• The evaluation lacks any human user study to corroborate the automated metrics with human judgments.

References  
[1] Rotian Luo et al. “Discriminability Objective for Training Descriptive Captions.” CVPR 2018.  
[2] Y.-C. Chen et al. “Distilling Knowledge Learned in BERT for Text Generation.” ACL 2020.  
[3] Qingzhong Wang et al. “Describing Like Humans: On Diversity in Image Captioning.” CVPR 2019.

Overall Assessment  
This paper delivers a strong baseline for novel-object captioning by integrating BERT and CLIP and achieves noteworthy empirical gains. However, the technical novelty is somewhat limited given the reliance on existing models and previously proposed objectives.  

Rating  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to improve novel object captioning i.e. describing objects/contents that are not seen during training. Specifically, the authors propose to correct the captions generated from captioning models using pre-trained BERT and CLIP models by rewarding the captions with precise and rich visual content. They show qualitative and quantitative improvements on CiDER and SPICE scores compared to previous work and propose to measure the fluency, fidelity and adequacy of the generated captions for a more comprehensive evaluation of novel object captioning.
reviews.main_review:
### Pros:
- Leveraging prior visual-linguistic knowledge from pre-trained BERT and CLIP models for the task of novel object captioning. The association between fidelity, fluency and adequacy with the training tasks of BERT and CLIP presents a good motivation for the method adopted. 

- The reward based loss functions designed for encouraging caption novelty and fluency and to discourage repetitive captions is interesting and novel. 

- The authors design fidelity/fluency and adequacy quantitative evaluation to measure the efficacy of their approach compared to previous work and show improvements. 

### Clarifications:
- Is the BERT model finetuned with the captions from the nocaps dataset in eq 2?  How is the CLIP condition satisfied in eq2, what happens if both the captions are unreliable ? Is there a threshold above which the CLIP scores are compared ?

- The BERT equations also could fulfil the fidelity objective because of the gating function. Is this observed?

- Are the improvements from Eq5. coming because of the r_CIDER term in the labelled caption data? Are the results in Table 1 compared with CIDER optimization for baselines? 

- What is observed if the reward is not calculated over the labeled training samples X_l?

- How will  the model perform if Eq 2 is performed during the inference stage only from a pre-trained captioning model?

- In fig 1, it is not very clear where the second caption “two glove hands...” comes from, is one from greedy decoding and other from sampling  ? 

- Are the novel words mostly objects/nouns?

### Cons: 

- While the method is effective and simple, there are certain ablations which could have been performed qualitatively and quantitatively to draw where the improvements come from. 

- The improvements in the method over the SOTA are via CIDER optimization on labeled examples in Eq 5. If the baselines are trained with CIDEr optimization, it might improve overall performance as well. 

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there? 

- As stated in the paper, that previous methods produce grammatically incorrect captions for NOC, that does not seem to be the case (eg in fig 4). There are details missing from baseline captions but they are still fluent.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is well written with detailed experimental results to support the claim. The paper proposes interesting ideas to improve NOC and is marginally novel but there are still some concerns as stated in the main review.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to improve novel object captioning i.e. describing objects/contents that are not seen during training. Specifically, the authors propose to correct the captions generated from captioning models using pre-trained BERT and CLIP models by rewarding the captions with precise and rich visual content. They show qualitative and quantitative improvements on CiDER and SPICE scores compared to previous work and propose to measure the fluency, fidelity and adequacy of the generated captions for a more comprehensive evaluation of novel object captioning.

Main Review:
### Pros:
- Leveraging prior visual-linguistic knowledge from pre-trained BERT and CLIP models for the task of novel object captioning. The association between fidelity, fluency and adequacy with the training tasks of BERT and CLIP presents a good motivation for the method adopted. 

- The reward based loss functions designed for encouraging caption novelty and fluency and to discourage repetitive captions is interesting and novel. 

- The authors design fidelity/fluency and adequacy quantitative evaluation to measure the efficacy of their approach compared to previous work and show improvements. 

### Clarifications:
- Is the BERT model finetuned with the captions from the nocaps dataset in eq 2?  How is the CLIP condition satisfied in eq2, what happens if both the captions are unreliable ? Is there a threshold above which the CLIP scores are compared ?

- The BERT equations also could fulfil the fidelity objective because of the gating function. Is this observed?

- Are the improvements from Eq5. coming because of the r_CIDER term in the labelled caption data? Are the results in Table 1 compared with CIDER optimization for baselines? 

- What is observed if the reward is not calculated over the labeled training samples X_l?

- How will  the model perform if Eq 2 is performed during the inference stage only from a pre-trained captioning model?

- In fig 1, it is not very clear where the second caption “two glove hands...” comes from, is one from greedy decoding and other from sampling  ? 

- Are the novel words mostly objects/nouns?

### Cons: 

- While the method is effective and simple, there are certain ablations which could have been performed qualitatively and quantitatively to draw where the improvements come from. 

- The improvements in the method over the SOTA are via CIDER optimization on labeled examples in Eq 5. If the baselines are trained with CIDEr optimization, it might improve overall performance as well. 

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there? 

- As stated in the paper, that previous methods produce grammatically incorrect captions for NOC, that does not seem to be the case (eg in fig 4). There are details missing from baseline captions but they are still fluent.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is well written with detailed experimental results to support the claim. The paper proposes interesting ideas to improve NOC and is marginally novel but there are still some concerns as stated in the main review.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This work tackles the challenge of novel object captioning, i.e., generating descriptions for visual objects not seen during training. The authors introduce a post-hoc correction mechanism for captions produced by existing captioning models. They leverage pre-trained BERT and CLIP to assign rewards that encourage more precise and content-rich visual descriptions. Empirical results demonstrate gains in CiDER and SPICE metrics over previous approaches. Additionally, the paper proposes a more holistic evaluation by quantifying fluency, fidelity, and adequacy of generated captions.

Main Review:

Pros:  
- The paper is well-motivated in drawing on the complementary strengths of BERT and CLIP’s pre-trained visual-linguistic representations to guide novel object captioning. The link between the models’ training objectives and the concepts of fidelity, fluency, and adequacy is persuasive.  
- The design of reward-based loss components to foster caption novelty and fluidity while penalizing repetitiveness is innovative.  
- The authors develop distinct quantitative measures for fidelity/fluency and adequacy, enabling a clearer demonstration of their approach’s benefits relative to prior work.

Clarification Requests:  
1. In Eq. 2, do you fine-tune the BERT model on the nocaps captions? How exactly is the CLIP constraint enforced—what happens if both BERT and CLIP give low confidence? Is there a threshold for comparing CLIP scores?  
2. Given the gating mechanism in the BERT-based reward, might BERT implicitly satisfy the fidelity criterion on its own? Has this effect been observed?  
3. Could the performance gains attributed to Eq. 5 simply arise from the r_CIDER term on labeled captions? Were the baseline methods also optimized with CIDEr during training?  
4. What are the results if you omit labeled samples X_l when computing the reward?  
5. How does the system behave if you apply Eq. 2 only at inference time, without retraining the captioning model?  
6. In Figure 1, it’s unclear what generates the second caption (“two glove hands…”). Is one the output of greedy decoding and the other from sampling?  
7. Are the newly introduced words predominantly object nouns?

Cons:  
- While the method is straightforward and effective, additional ablation studies—both qualitative and quantitative—would help pinpoint the exact source(s) of improvement.  
- The reported gains over the state of the art appear driven by CIDEr optimization on labeled data (Eq. 5). If the baselines were similarly trained with CIDEr, they might achieve comparable boosts.  
- Although the focus is novel object captioning, enhancing fluency and adequacy is a general concern in image captioning. Could this approach be applied more broadly?  
- The paper claims prior NOC methods generate ungrammatical captions, yet Figure 4 shows baseline outputs that are fluent despite lacking some detail.

Summary of the Review:  
Overall, the manuscript is clearly written and backed by detailed experiments. The ideas for leveraging BERT and CLIP as post-hoc caption correctors are compelling and represent a modest advancement in novel object captioning. A few open questions and additional analyses remain, but these do not overshadow the paper’s contributions.

Rating:  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

- The authors design fidelity/fluency and adequacy quantitative evaluation to measure the efficacy of their approach compared to previous work and show improvements. 

### Clarifications:
- Is the BERT model finetuned with the captions from the nocaps dataset in eq 2?  How is the CLIP condition satisfied in eq2, what happens if both the captions are unreliable ? Is there a threshold above which the CLIP scores are compared ?

- The BERT equations also could fulfil the fidelity objective because of the gating function. Is this observed?

- Are the improvements from Eq5. coming because of the r_CIDER term in the labelled caption data? Are the results in Table 1 compared with CIDER optimization for baselines? 

- What is observed if the reward is not calculated over the labeled training samples X_l?

- How will  the model perform if Eq 2 is performed during the inference stage only from a pre-trained captioning model?

- In fig 1, it is not very clear where the second caption “two glove hands...” comes from, is one from greedy decoding and other from sampling  ? 

- Are the novel words mostly objects/nouns?

### Cons: 

- While the method is effective and simple, there are certain ablations which could have been performed qualitatively and quantitatively to draw where the improvements come from. 

- The improvements in the method over the SOTA are via CIDER optimization on labeled examples in Eq 5. If the baselines are trained with CIDEr optimization, it might improve overall performance as well. 

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there? 

- As stated in the paper, that previous methods produce grammatically incorrect captions for NOC, that does not seem to be the case (eg in fig 4). There are details missing from baseline captions but they are still fluent.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is well written with detailed experimental results to support the claim. The paper proposes interesting ideas to improve NOC and is marginally novel but there are still some concerns as stated in the main review.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The papers presents an approach for the task of novel object captioning (NOC). NOC requires the model to describe novel objects appearing in test images that were not described in the training captions. The authors claim that existing methods are not good at the task because they don't tackle three concepts required to produce a good caption — 

1) Fluency: generating human-like captions, 

2) Fidelity: whether the caption is describing the novel object in the image or not, 

3) Adequacy: whether the caption covers all the salient visual concepts in the image. 

The authors propose a model Visual-Linguistic Adequacy, Fidelity, and Adequacy (VLAF2) to address these issues. The model utilizes BERT to sample different variations of the captions by substituting random words from the caption. It uses CLIP to assess which of these are more relevant to the image. The model also uses CLIP score and combine it with CIDEr based reward to further train the captioning model via reinforce. 

The model is evaluated on the nocaps benchmark on which it achieves improvements over existing sota methods like VINVL+VIVO. The authors also show that apart from captioning metrics, the model also does better on fluency (B@4, C), Fidelity (Precision), Adequacy(Recall).
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths

1. Overall, I concur with the key insight from the paper which is that models for NOC should tackle fluency, fidelity and adequacy. It has been observed in Agrawal et al 2019 (nocaps dataset) that methods suffer from these issues. Captions which are fluent often aren't accurate, and captions which are accurate often aren't fluent.
2. The authors take reasonable steps to fix these issues. Using a LM to fix fluency is a good idea. The authors address one of the main issues with such an approach — BERT like language models can produce fluent captions but it's hard to judge their accuracy. The authors use CLIP to score these and select the best method. Such an approach makes sense.
3. Another novel contribution in this work is using uncaptioned images (images in the OpenImages dataset which do not have associated training captions) during training. Since these don't have training captions, they modify the reward to use CLIP as well penalize repeated words. Indeed, they show that CLIP + repeated penalty is helpful for overall performance (Table 3).

### Weaknesses

1. One of the biggest and most important weakness in the paper is that the paper doesn't follow the guidelines from the nocaps paper. As mentioned in the nocaps paper (section 3.3), "Do not use additional paired image-caption data collected from humans. Improving evaluation scores by leveraging additional human-generated paired imagecaption data is antithetical to this benchmark – the only paired image-caption dataset that should be used is the COCO Captions 2017 training split. However, external text corpora, knowledge bases, and object detection datasets may be used during training or inference". The paper heavily relies on CLIP which has been trained on alt-text data for images (which are human written) for improving the captioning performance. This makes the method proposed in the paper not comparable to existing methods in my opinion. The authors should have used the nocaps (XD) for evaluation.
2. Related to the second point, I would have liked to see if you can replace CLIP with other approaches (ViLBERT) which also outputs a score indicating how well the caption is aligned with the image. This would form a fair comparison.
3. Since CLIP is trained on alt-text data for images, it would be interesting to see if joint-training on Conceptual Captions (or a similar web-scale data) works better instead.
4. CLIP is being used for checking the relevance of the caption with the image. I wonder how will other similar metrics for caption relevance (VIFIDEL) compare against scores from CLIP.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I think the paper presents an interesting insight (breaking down caption quality into fluency, adequacy and fidelity) and presents an approach which improves all the three aspects on the nocaps dataset. I raised a couple of concerns (use of CLIP which has been trained on human-generated alt-text data) which goes against the guidelines of the benchmark. It nullifies some of the claims in the paper and I'd be interested in hearing from the authors regarding that. I would have also liked to see comparison with other caption relevance metrics like VIFIDEL which would make the contributions stronger. Overall, I am borderline on this paper and will update my thoughts based on the author response.

NOC requires the model to describe novel objects appearing in test images that were not described in the training captions. The authors claim that existing methods are not good at the task because they don't tackle three concepts required to produce a good caption —

The model is evaluated on the nocaps benchmark on which it achieves improvements over existing sota methods like VINVL+VIVO. The authors also show that apart from captioning metrics, the model also does better on fluency (B@4, C), Fidelity (Precision), Adequacy(Recall).

Main Review:
### Strengths

1. Overall, I concur with the key insight from the paper which is that models for NOC should tackle fluency, fidelity and adequacy. The authors address one of the main issues with such an approach — BERT like language models can produce fluent captions but it's hard to judge their accuracy. 1. One of the biggest and most important weakness in the paper is that the paper doesn't follow the guidelines from the nocaps paper. As mentioned in the nocaps paper (section 3.3), "Do not use additional paired image-caption data collected from humans. Improving evaluation scores by leveraging additional human-generated paired imagecaption data is antithetical to this benchmark – the only paired image-caption dataset that should be used is the COCO Captions 2017 training split. However, external text corpora, knowledge bases, and object detection datasets may be used during training or inference". This makes the method proposed in the paper not comparable to existing methods in my opinion. The authors should have used the nocaps (XD) for evaluation.

I raised a couple of concerns (use of CLIP which has been trained on human-generated alt-text data) which goes against the guidelines of the benchmark. reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This manuscript tackles Novel Object Captioning (NOC), where a model must describe objects in test images that never appeared in training captions. The authors argue that successful NOC models must balance three criteria:  
1) Fluency: producing natural, human-like sentences;  
2) Fidelity: ensuring the caption correctly identifies the novel object in the image;  
3) Adequacy: covering all key visual elements.  

To address these, they introduce VLAF² (Visual-Linguistic Adequacy, Fidelity, and Fluency). Their system first generates multiple caption variants by perturbing words via BERT, then employs CLIP to rank these variants by image relevance. Finally, they combine the CLIP score with a CIDEr-based reward and fine-tune the captioning model using REINFORCE. Evaluated on the nocaps benchmark, VLAF² outperforms leading methods such as VINVL+VIVO, and shows gains not only in traditional caption metrics but also in fluency (BLEU-4, CIDEr), fidelity (Precision), and adequacy (Recall).

Strengths:  
1. I share the authors’ central insight that NOC requires joint optimization of fluency, fidelity, and adequacy. Previous work on nocaps (Agrawal et al., 2019) has highlighted that fluent outputs often lack accuracy, and vice versa.  
2. The approach is well motivated: leveraging a language model to enhance fluency is sensible, and pairing it with CLIP to ensure visual accuracy addresses a known drawback of LM-only methods.  
3. Introducing uncaptioned images from OpenImages during training is a clever advance. Since these images lack ground-truth captions, the authors adapt their reward to rely on CLIP and add a penalty for repeated words. Table 3 convincingly demonstrates that this CLIP-plus-repetition penalty yields measurable performance benefits.

Weaknesses:  
1. The paper contravenes the nocaps benchmark guidelines (Sec. 3.3 of Agrawal et al. 2019), which forbid using extra human-paired image–caption data beyond COCO Captions. CLIP itself is trained on large-scale alt-text, effectively human-written captions. This reliance on external alt-text undermines comparability to prior nocaps methods. The authors should instead evaluate on the nocaps “XD” split or otherwise justify compliance.  
2. It would strengthen the work to see whether other vision–language scoring models (e.g., ViLBERT) can substitute for CLIP, providing a fairer baseline comparison.  
3. Given CLIP’s pretraining on alt-text, one wonders if joint training with a web-scale caption corpus like Conceptual Captions might yield similar or superior results.  
4. Beyond CLIP, the authors could compare with alternative caption–image relevance metrics such as VIFIDEL to assess whether CLIP’s ranking is uniquely beneficial.

Overall Assessment:  
The paper offers a valuable decomposition of caption quality into fluency, fidelity, and adequacy and presents a coherent method that advances performance on nocaps. My main concern is the use of CLIP (trained on human alt-text), which conflicts with the benchmark’s data restrictions and may invalidate direct comparisons. Additional experiments—replacing CLIP with other scorers or exploring web-scale caption pretraining—would bolster the claims. I remain on the fence and look forward to the authors’ response to these critiques.

Rating:  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
